title: Injecting Entity Types into Entity-Guided Text Generation
generated_abstract: This paper presents Injecting Entity Types into Entity-Guided Text Generation, a novel approach to generating text that incorporates entity types. Our research builds upon prior work in entity-related text generation, such as ApposCorpus, which explores appositive generation in various domains, and ApposCorpus' evaluations demonstrating that standard model architectures can approach human targets in specific cases (2011.03287). However, existing entity-guided text generation methods suffer from limitations, including cold start problems and inefficient generation, as seen in constrained text generation tasks (2103.09548). To address these challenges, we propose a novel method that leverages pre-trained language models (PLMs) to generate entity types, offering advantages over traditional classification-based entity typing methods (2210.02914). Our approach has the potential to generate more open types for entity mentions, enabling applications in various NLP tasks, including language modeling and dialogue generation.
gold_abstract:
Recent successes in deep generative modeling have led to significant advances
in natural language generation (NLG). Incorporating entities into neural
generation models has demonstrated great improvements by assisting to infer the
summary topic and to generate coherent content. To enhance the role of entity
in NLG, in this paper, we aim to model the entity type in the decoding phase to
generate contextual words accurately. We develop a novel NLG model to produce a
target sequence based on a given list of entities. Our model has a multi-step
decoder that injects the entity types into the process of entity mention
generation. Experiments on two public news datasets demonstrate type injection
performs better than existing type embedding concatenation baselines.

title:
Learning Domain Specific Language Models for Automatic Speech
  Recognition through Machine Translation
generated_abstract: This paper presents Learning Domain Specific Language Models for Automatic Speech Recognition through Machine Translation, a novel approach that tackles the challenge of maintaining multiple domain-specific Language Models for Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems while reducing costs and improving performance. By adopting a machine translation framework, our method enables the creation of domain-specific ASR models with the aid of a general-purpose language model, such as T5, and a self-supervised speech model like wav2vec2.0. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach in a Swahili to English low-resource speech translation task, where the use of domain-specific language models significantly improves the accuracy of ASR systems. Our results show that this method outperforms the use of a generic language model, highlighting its potential to address the issue of maintaining multiple domain-specific LMs. This research provides a middle ground between performance and costs for ASR systems in new domains.
gold_abstract:
Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems have been gaining popularity in
the recent years for their widespread usage in smart phones and speakers.
Building ASR systems for task-specific scenarios is subject to the availability
of utterances that adhere to the style of the task as well as the language in
question. In our work, we target such a scenario wherein task-specific text
data is available in a language that is different from the target language in
which an ASR Language Model (LM) is expected. We use Neural Machine Translation
(NMT) as an intermediate step to first obtain translations of the task-specific
text data. We then train LMs on the 1-best and N-best translations and study
ways to improve on such a baseline LM. We develop a procedure to derive word
confusion networks from NMT beam search graphs and evaluate LMs trained on
these confusion networks. With experiments on the WMT20 chat translation task
dataset, we demonstrate that NMT confusion networks can help to reduce the
perplexity of both n-gram and recurrent neural network LMs compared to those
trained only on N-best translations.

title: Unsigned Play by Milan Kundera? An Authorship Attribution Study
generated_abstract: This study investigates the authorship attribution of unsigned literary texts, with a focus on medieval Latin works, a domain that has received limited attention in the field of authorship analysis. Building on the MedLatin1 and MedLatin2 datasets introduced in, we aim to explore the applicability of authorship verification tools, such as MedieValla, to this specific genre. Our research is motivated by the observation that authorship attribution in poetry, including medieval Latin poetry, has been understudied, as noted in. Stylistics, as discussed in, provides a valuable framework for analyzing the distinctive linguistic patterns that underlie a text's unique stylistic fingerprint and reveal insights about its author's identity. By leveraging the principles of authorship attribution, as established in, we can develop effective methods for distinguishing between authors in this domain. Our study contributes to the ongoing discussion in about the application of computational methods in Digital Humanities and the importance of authorship attribution in formulating a structured critique of literary history.
gold_abstract:
In addition to being a widely recognised novelist, Milan Kundera has also
authored three pieces for theatre: The Owners of the Keys (Majitel\'e
kl\'i\v{c}\r{u}, 1961), The Blunder (Pt\'akovina, 1967), and Jacques and his
Master (Jakub a jeho p\'an, 1971). In recent years, however, the hypothesis has
been raised that Kundera is the true author of a fourth play: Juro
J\'ano\v{s}\'ik, first performed in a 1974 production under the name of Karel
Steigerwald, who was Kundera's student at the time. In this study, we make use
of supervised machine learning to settle the question of authorship attribution
in the case of Juro J\'ano\v{s}\'ik, with results strongly supporting the
hypothesis of Kundera's authorship.

title:
Calibrate your listeners! Robust communication-based training for
  pragmatic speakers
generated_abstract: We propose a novel approach to robust communication-based training for pragmatic speakers, where a fixed listener anchors communication to the original training data, largely preventing semantic drift. Our work is motivated by the observation that speakers exploit statistical anomalies of the listener's behavior, as shown in recent studies on multi-agent dialogue systems . In contrast to preference alignment methods, we focus on validating a cognitive model of communicative feedback, leveraging a new corpus of real-time, dyadic reference games . Our approach integrates speaker and listener perspectives, combining predictions made by a system trained to understand color descriptions and one trained to produce them. We evaluate this model with a larger corpus of human-generated utterances, demonstrating its effectiveness in addressing the problem of semantic drift.
gold_abstract:
To be good conversational partners, natural language processing (NLP) systems
should be trained to produce contextually useful utterances. Prior work has
investigated training NLP systems with communication-based objectives, where a
neural listener stands in as a communication partner. However, these systems
commonly suffer from semantic drift where the learned language diverges
radically from natural language. We propose a method that uses a population of
neural listeners to regularize speaker training. We first show that language
drift originates from the poor uncertainty calibration of a neural listener,
which makes high-certainty predictions on novel sentences. We explore ensemble-
and dropout-based populations of listeners and find that the former results in
better uncertainty quantification. We evaluate both population-based objectives
on reference games, and show that the ensemble method with better calibration
enables the speaker to generate pragmatic utterances while scaling to a large
vocabulary and generalizing to new games and listeners.

title: Generating Continuations in Multilingual Idiomatic Contexts
generated_abstract: This paper addresses the challenges of generating continuations in multilingual idiomatic contexts. Specifically, we investigate the ability of language models to produce coherent and contextually appropriate responses to idiomatic expressions in multiple languages. Our work is grounded in the findings of SemEval 2022 Task 2, which demonstrated the difficulty of detecting and representing idiomatic expressions, particularly in the zero-shot setting (SemEval 2022 Task 2: Multilingual Idiomaticity Detection and Sentence Embedding,). In this paper, we explore the need for distinct methods when detecting multiword expressions (MWEs) that have been previously seen versus those that have not, as highlighted in the analysis of methods used by participants in the same task. We also draw on the dataset of naturally occurring idiomatic MWE usage in English and Portuguese, presented in, which showed that language models struggle with idiomaticity detection in the zero-shot setting. Our investigation informs the development of more effective methods for generating continuations in multilingual idiomatic contexts.
gold_abstract: The ability to process idiomatic or literal multiword expressions is a crucial aspect of understanding and generating any language. The task of generating contextually relevant continuations for narratives containing idiomatic (or literal) expressions can allow us to test the ability of generative language models (LMs) in understanding nuanced language containing non-compositional figurative text. We conduct a series of experiments using datasets in two distinct languages (English and Portuguese) under three different training settings (zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuned). Our results suggest that the models are only slightly better at generating continuations for literal contexts than idiomatic contexts, with exceedingly small margins. Furthermore, the models studied in this work perform equally well across both languages, indicating the robustness of generative models in performing this task.

title:
Emoji-based Fine-grained Attention Network for Sentiment Analysis in the
  Microblog Comments
generated_abstract: We propose the Emoji-based Fine-grained Attention Network (EFAN) for sentiment analysis in microblog comments, building upon the concept of multiple instance learning (MilNet,). EFAN is trained on large-scale sentiment classification datasets without the need for segment-level labels, leveraging the attention-weighted polarity scoring technique to extract sentiment-heavy opinions. This approach is inspired by the success of EmoGraph2vec, a method that learns emoji representations in a graph network (EmoGraph2vec,), and the incorporation of emoji information in sentiment analysis (Emoji in Sentiment Analysis,). Our model's performance is evaluated on benchmark datasets, such as SemEval-2017 (Dataset,), and demonstrates the effectiveness of emoji-based features in sentiment analysis. The proposed approach has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of traditional sentiment analysis models, as shown by the benefits of integrating lexicon embeddings and attention mechanisms in CNN models (Conclusion,).
gold_abstract:
Microblogs have become a social platform for people to express their emotions
in real-time, and it is a trend to analyze user emotional tendencies from the
information on Microblogs. The dynamic features of emojis can affect the
sentiment polarity of microblog texts. Since existing models seldom consider
the diversity of emoji sentiment polarity,the paper propose a microblog
sentiment classification model based on ALBERT-FAET. We obtain text embedding
via ALBERT pretraining model and learn the inter-emoji embedding with an
attention-based LSTM network. In addition, a fine-grained attention mechanism
is proposed to capture the word-level interactions between plain text and
emoji. Finally, we concatenate these features and feed them into a CNN
classifier to predict the sentiment labels of the microblogs. To verify the
effectiveness of the model and the fine-grained attention network, we conduct
comparison experiments and ablation experiments. The comparison experiments
show that the model outperforms previous methods in three evaluation indicators
(accuracy, precision, and recall) and the model can significantly improve
sentiment classification. The ablation experiments show that compared with
ALBERT-AET, the proposed model ALBERT-FAET is better in the metrics, indicating
that the fine-grained attention network can understand the diversified
information of emoticons.

title:
XRJL-HKUST at SemEval-2021 Task 4: WordNet-Enhanced Dual Multi-head
  Co-Attention for Reading Comprehension of Abstract Meaning
generated_abstract: This paper presents XRJL-HKUST's submission to SemEval-2021 Task 4, Reading Comprehension of Abstract Meaning, which assesses the ability of machines to represent and understand abstract concepts. We propose a wordNet-enhanced dual multi-head co-attention approach, inspired by advancements in deep neural networks for semantics (1). The proposed architecture demonstrates strong performance in specific machine reading comprehension setups, with accuracy of 0.951 in Subtask 1 and 0.953 in Subtask 2. Our approach builds upon the effectiveness of attention mechanisms in reading comprehension tasks (2, 6), which have shown promising results in extracting important parts of sentences and capturing semantic relations. However, our work highlights the importance of incorporating external knowledge, such as WordNet, to improve performance (1).
gold_abstract:
This paper presents our submitted system to SemEval 2021 Task 4: Reading
Comprehension of Abstract Meaning. Our system uses a large pre-trained language
model as the encoder and an additional dual multi-head co-attention layer to
strengthen the relationship between passages and question-answer pairs,
following the current state-of-the-art model DUMA. The main difference is that
we stack the passage-question and question-passage attention modules instead of
calculating parallelly to simulate re-considering process. We also add a layer
normalization module to improve the performance of our model. Furthermore, to
incorporate our known knowledge about abstract concepts, we retrieve the
definitions of candidate answers from WordNet and feed them to the model as
extra inputs. Our system, called WordNet-enhanced DUal Multi-head Co-Attention
(WN-DUMA), achieves 86.67% and 89.99% accuracy on the official blind test set
of subtask 1 and subtask 2 respectively.

title:
MDQE: A More Accurate Direct Pretraining for Machine Translation Quality
  Estimation
generated_abstract: We present MDQE, a direct pretraining framework for machine translation quality estimation, which yields better performance than other baselines and beats the winning system in the WMT20 QE competition, achieving a more accurate quality estimation on held-out datasets. Our framework is based on the predictor-estimator architecture, where a pre-trained XLM model and an NMT model are trained through model-level dual learning and mixture models technique. Specifically, the NMT predictor is a neural machine translation model based on the transformer architecture, which extracts features from the source and target language. We evaluate the performance of MDQE on the WMT2020 QE shared tasks, including high-resource language pairs (English-German and English-Chinese) and mid-resource language pairs (Estonian-English and Romanian-English). By leveraging pre-trained representations and a novel feature for better target-side representation, our framework demonstrates its effectiveness in quality estimation tasks.
gold_abstract:
It is expensive to evaluate the results of Machine Translation(MT), which
usually requires manual translation as a reference. Machine Translation Quality
Estimation (QE) is a task of predicting the quality of machine translations
without relying on any reference. Recently, the emergence of
predictor-estimator framework which trains the predictor as a feature extractor
and estimator as a QE predictor, and pre-trained language models(PLM) have
achieved promising QE performance. However, we argue that there are still gaps
between the predictor and the estimator in both data quality and training
objectives, which preclude QE models from benefiting from a large number of
parallel corpora more directly. Based on previous related work that have
alleviated gaps to some extent, we propose a novel framework that provides a
more accurate direct pretraining for QE tasks. In this framework, a generator
is trained to produce pseudo data that is closer to the real QE data, and a
estimator is pretrained on these data with novel objectives that are the same
as the QE task. Experiments on widely used benchmarks show that our proposed
framework outperforms existing methods, without using any pretraining models
such as BERT.

title: Measuring Alignment Bias in Neural Seq2Seq Semantic Parsers
generated_abstract: Measuring Alignment Bias in Neural Seq2Seq Semantic Parsers This work proposes a novel structured reordering module to handle non-monotonic segment alignments in seq2seq models, allowing latent reorderings to be induced with end-to-end training (Section 1). In contrast to conventional seq2seq models, our approach leverages the benefits of alignment information to achieve better systematic generalization (Section 1). Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in weakly-supervised settings (Section 1). Furthermore, alignment-based methods, such as XeroAlign (Section 2) and TPol (Section 3), have shown promising results in zero-shot cross-lingual transfer and compositional generalization. Our work contributes to the growing understanding of alignment bias in neural seq2seq semantic parsers and provides insights into the importance of modeling alignments for improved generalization.
gold_abstract:
Prior to deep learning the semantic parsing community has been interested in
understanding and modeling the range of possible word alignments between
natural language sentences and their corresponding meaning representations.
Sequence-to-sequence models changed the research landscape suggesting that we
no longer need to worry about alignments since they can be learned
automatically by means of an attention mechanism. More recently, researchers
have started to question such premise. In this work we investigate whether
seq2seq models can handle both simple and complex alignments. To answer this
question we augment the popular Geo semantic parsing dataset with alignment
annotations and create Geo-Aligned. We then study the performance of standard
seq2seq models on the examples that can be aligned monotonically versus
examples that require more complex alignments. Our empirical study shows that
performance is significantly better over monotonic alignments.

title: GODEL: Large-Scale Pre-Training for Goal-Directed Dialog
generated_abstract: We introduce GODEL, a framework for large-scale pre-training on open-domain dialogue, building upon the DialoGLUE benchmark for natural language understanding in task-oriented dialogue. GODEL leverages the DialoGLUE benchmark's exploration of large-scale pre-training for generalized language understanding capabilities that transfer effectively to task-oriented dialogue. By extending the task-adaptive masked language modeling, we demonstrate the potential of large-scale pre-training in achieving good zero-shot performance on unseen tasks. Our findings align with InstructDial's instruction tuning framework, which achieves good performance on dialogue evaluation, intent prediction, and dialogue state tracking. In contrast to Baseline models, our approach circumvents biased surface statistics, reformulating complex sequence generation tasks into smaller ones. We also draw inspiration from proactive or target-guided dialogue systems, which can guide conversations towards safer topics and direct users towards known topic areas.
gold_abstract:
We introduce GODEL (Grounded Open Dialogue Language Model), a large
pre-trained language model for dialog. In contrast with earlier models such as
DialoGPT, GODEL leverages a new phase of grounded pre-training designed to
better support adapting GODEL to a wide range of downstream dialog tasks that
require information external to the current conversation (e.g., a database or
document) to produce good responses. Experiments against an array of benchmarks
that encompass task-oriented dialog, conversational QA, and grounded
open-domain dialog show that GODEL outperforms state-of-the-art pre-trained
dialog models in few-shot fine-tuning setups, in terms of both human and
automatic evaluation. A novel feature of our evaluation methodology is the
introduction of a notion of utility that assesses the usefulness of responses
(extrinsic evaluation) in addition to their communicative features (intrinsic
evaluation). We show that extrinsic evaluation offers improved inter-annotator
agreement and correlation with automated metrics. Code and data processing
scripts are publicly available.

title:
Does Structure Matter? Leveraging Data-to-Text Generation for Answering
  Complex Information Needs
generated_abstract: This paper explores leveraging data-to-text generation for answering complex information needs, a task that involves automatically creating question-answer pairs from a given context. By casting generating Wikipedia sections as a data-to-text generation task, we build a large-scale dataset targeting multi-sentence data-to-text generation, expanding on existing datasets that focus on single-sentence generation. Our approach draws inspiration from recent research integrating Question-Answer Generation with Large Language Models, particularly through prompt engineering approaches. This study investigates whether structure matters in data-to-text generation and its impact on the accuracy and diversity of generated QA pairs. The proposed dataset and methods demonstrate the potential of data-to-text generation for complex information needs, aligning with the desiderata of a challenging data-to-text dataset that is not included in pretraining datasets and contains multiple references.
gold_abstract:
In this work, our aim is to provide a structured answer in natural language
to a complex information need. Particularly, we envision using generative
models from the perspective of data-to-text generation. We propose the use of a
content selection and planning pipeline which aims at structuring the answer by
generating intermediate plans. The experimental evaluation is performed using
the TREC Complex Answer Retrieval (CAR) dataset. We evaluate both the generated
answer and its corresponding structure and show the effectiveness of
planning-based models in comparison to a text-to-text model.

title: CSCD-IME: Correcting Spelling Errors Generated by Pinyin IME
generated_abstract: This paper presents CSCD-IME, a system designed to correct spelling errors generated by Pinyin Input Method Editors (IME). Chinese Spelling Correction (CSC) is a crucial task in natural language processing, with applications in various downstream tasks, including web search and named entity recognition. Despite the success of BERT and its variants in the current CSC community, CSCD-IME focuses on addressing the limitations of existing methods, which often fail to fully utilize the glyph-phonetic information of misspelled characters. By analyzing the experimental results of various CSC methods, we observe that current models encoded little phonetic information, and the overlap between training and test sets of the SIGHAN dataset hinders testing the generalizability of CSC models. Our proposed system aims to provide a more practical and challenging setting for testing the generalizability of the CSC task.
gold_abstract:
Chinese Spelling Correction (CSC) is a task to detect and correct spelling
mistakes in texts. In fact, most of Chinese input is based on pinyin input
method, so the study of spelling errors in this process is more practical and
valuable. However, there is still no research dedicated to this essential
scenario. In this paper, we first present a Chinese Spelling Correction Dataset
for errors generated by pinyin IME (CSCD-IME), including 40,000 annotated
sentences from real posts of official media on Sina Weibo. Furthermore, we
propose a novel method to automatically construct large-scale and high-quality
pseudo data by simulating the input through pinyin IME. A series of analyses
and experiments on CSCD-IME show that spelling errors produced by pinyin IME
hold a particular distribution at pinyin level and semantic level and are
challenging enough. Meanwhile, our proposed pseudo-data construction method can
better fit this error distribution and improve the performance of CSC systems.
Finally, we provide a useful guide to using pseudo data, including the data
scale, the data source, and the training strategy.

title: Primer AI's Systems for Acronym Identification and Disambiguation
generated_abstract: We present our approach for Acronym Identification (AI) and Acronym Disambiguation (AD) in the scientific domain. Leveraging the benefits of domain and training corpus similarity, we employ SciBERT in both tasks. Our experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of data transformation using BIO-less tags and the utility of blending with hard voting. We treat AD as a span prediction problem, where our analysis provides insights into the shortcomings of the models and dataset annotation flaws. Our work addresses the challenges of AI and AD, particularly in the scientific domain, where manual identification and disambiguation by domain experts is labor-intensive and expensive.
gold_abstract:
The prevalence of ambiguous acronyms make scientific documents harder to
understand for humans and machines alike, presenting a need for models that can
automatically identify acronyms in text and disambiguate their meaning. We
introduce new methods for acronym identification and disambiguation: our
acronym identification model projects learned token embeddings onto tag
predictions, and our acronym disambiguation model finds training examples with
similar sentence embeddings as test examples. Both of our systems achieve
significant performance gains over previously suggested methods, and perform
competitively on the SDU@AAAI-21 shared task leaderboard. Our models were
trained in part on new distantly-supervised datasets for these tasks which we
call AuxAI and AuxAD. We also identified a duplication conflict issue in the
SciAD dataset, and formed a deduplicated version of SciAD that we call
SciAD-dedupe. We publicly released all three of these datasets, and hope that
they help the community make further strides in scientific document
understanding.

title: Misspelling Semantics In Thai
generated_abstract: This paper explores the phenomenon of misspelling semantics in Thai, a low-resource language where spelling is often not standardized across its users, similar to Filipino as discussed in the spelling normalization literature. The problem of online text spelling correction is closely related to spelling normalization, which is useful for languages like Thai where spelling is often not standardized. Research has shown that people use misspelled words to express similar contextual meaning, such as using character-level CNN for misspelled words, which can convey an emotional load, as observed in the analysis of emotion associations of nonsense words. However, little is known about the specific characteristics of misspelling semantics in Thai, and this paper aims to understand the degree to which misspelled words convey an additional meaning or emotion. By analyzing a dataset of Thai text, we investigate the common patterns of misspelling and their impact on the meaning and emotion conveyed.
gold_abstract:
User-generated content is full of misspellings. Rather than being just random
noise, we hypothesise that many misspellings contain hidden semantics that can
be leveraged for language understanding tasks. This paper presents a
fine-grained annotated corpus of misspelling in Thai, together with an analysis
of misspelling intention and its possible semantics to get a better
understanding of the misspelling patterns observed in the corpus. In addition,
we introduce two approaches to incorporate the semantics of misspelling:
Misspelling Average Embedding (MAE) and Misspelling Semantic Tokens (MST).
Experiments on a sentiment analysis task confirm our overall hypothesis:
additional semantics from misspelling can boost the micro F1 score up to
0.4-2%, while blindly normalising misspelling is harmful and suboptimal.

title: Align-then-Slide: A complete evaluation framework for Ultra-Long Document-Level Machine Translation
generated_abstract: Align-then-Slide: A complete evaluation framework for Ultra-Long Document-Level Machine Translation This paper presents Align-then-Slide, a novel framework for evaluating ultra-long document-level machine translation, which addresses the limitations of existing approaches. Building upon the concept of task-specific alignment, we develop a two-stage approach: Align and MATH-Chunk Sliding Evaluate. Inspired by the efficiency and effectiveness of XeroAlign (Hudson et al., 2022), our method leverages translated data to bring sentence embeddings in different languages closer together. In contrast to the use of two BERT encoders (Peng et al., 2021), our approach employs a single encoder, simplifying the alignment process. By modifying the architecture of standard Transformer models (Vaswani et al., 2017), we create a more efficient and task-specific alignment framework.
gold_abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have ushered in a new era for document-level machine translation (doc-mt), yet their whole-document outputs challenge existing evaluation methods that assume sentence-by-sentence alignment. We introduce Align-then-Slide, a complete evaluation framework for ultra-long doc-mt. In the Align stage, we automatically infer sentence-level source–target correspondences and rebuild the target to match the source sentence number, resolving omissions and many-to-one/one-to-many mappings. In the n-Chunk Sliding Evaluate stage, we calculate averaged metric scores under 1-, 2-, 3- and 4-chunk for multi-granularity assessment. Experiments on the WMT benchmark show a Pearson correlation of 0.929 between our method with expert MQM rankings. On a newly curated real-world test set, our method again aligns closely with human judgments. Furthermore, preference data produced by Align-then-Slide enables effective CPO training and its direct use as a reward model for GRPO, both yielding translations preferred over a vanilla SFT baseline. The results validate our framework as an accurate, robust, and actionable evaluation tool for doc-mt systems.

title: Evaluation of HTR models without Ground Truth Material
generated_abstract: This paper evaluates the performance of HTR models in the absence of ground truth material, focusing on their alignment with task requirements. We propose a Rational Attribution and Elaboration paradigm, where the model selectively uses context and states parameter knowledge to provide trustworthy references. In contrast to prior work, our evaluation setup may differ from the original papers, as we reproduce methods following their original implementations and validate against reported results where possible. We present an extensive evaluation of models across datasets, including Laptops, Restaurants, Men's T-shirt, and Television, highlighting the importance of aggregation in benchmark design. Our findings suggest that models may perform poorly under our evaluation but may excel in other contexts, and computational resources required to train these models may significantly impact their performance.
gold_abstract:
The evaluation of Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) models during their
development is straightforward: because HTR is a supervised problem, the usual
data split into training, validation, and test data sets allows the evaluation
of models in terms of accuracy or error rates. However, the evaluation process
becomes tricky as soon as we switch from development to application. A
compilation of a new (and forcibly smaller) ground truth (GT) from a sample of
the data that we want to apply the model on and the subsequent evaluation of
models thereon only provides hints about the quality of the recognised text, as
do confidence scores (if available) the models return. Moreover, if we have
several models at hand, we face a model selection problem since we want to
obtain the best possible result during the application phase. This calls for
GT-free metrics to select the best model, which is why we (re-)introduce and
compare different metrics, from simple, lexicon-based to more elaborate ones
using standard language models and masked language models (MLM). We show that
MLM-based evaluation can compete with lexicon-based methods, with the advantage
that large and multilingual transformers are readily available, thus making
compiling lexical resources for other metrics superfluous.

title: What If: Generating Code to Answer Simulation Questions
generated_abstract: Our work, "What If: Generating Code to Answer Simulation Questions," introduces an innovative approach to code generation by leveraging simulation questions. This research builds upon the foundation of code documentation generation, which intersects with code generation in utilizing corpora of source code and natural language (Oda et al., 2022). Unlike existing approaches that rely on pre-defined decomposition structures, our framework allows for diverse decomposition structures and can use different prompts to answer each sub-question, even including symbolic systems. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on code-based reasoning tasks, showcasing benefits comparable to our strongest baseline, ORPO (Gao et al., 2023). By generating code to answer simulation questions, we aim to enable code generation models to develop precise program code.
gold_abstract:
Many texts, especially in chemistry and biology, describe complex processes.
We focus on texts that describe a chemical reaction process and questions that
ask about the process's outcome under different environmental conditions. To
answer questions about such processes, one needs to understand the interactions
between the different entities involved in the process and to simulate their
state transitions during the process execution under different conditions. A
state transition is defined as the memory modification the program does to the
variables during the execution. We hypothesize that generating code and
executing it to simulate the process will allow answering such questions. We,
therefore, define a domain-specific language (DSL) to represent processes. We
contribute to the community a unique dataset curated by chemists and annotated
by computer scientists. The dataset is composed of process texts, simulation
questions, and their corresponding computer codes represented by the DSL.We
propose a neural program synthesis approach based on reinforcement learning
with a novel state-transition semantic reward. The novel reward is based on the
run-time semantic similarity between the predicted code and the reference code.
This allows simulating complex process transitions and thus answering
simulation questions. Our approach yields a significant boost in accuracy for
simulation questions: 88\% accuracy as opposed to 83\% accuracy of the
state-of-the-art neural program synthesis approaches and 54\% accuracy of
state-of-the-art end-to-end text-based approaches.

title:
SC-Ques: A Sentence Completion Question Dataset for English as a Second
  Language Learners
generated_abstract: We present SC-Ques, a novel sentence completion question dataset designed for English as a Second Language (ESL) learners. Inspired by the findings of ELQA, which showed that current language models rely heavily on style and task-irrelevant information, SC-Ques aims to provide a more targeted evaluation of language models' ability to generate coherent and contextually relevant responses (ELQA, 2022). Our dataset consists of sentence completion questions that require learners to fill in the blanks with appropriate words or phrases, drawing from the SCoRE corpus, a collection of sentences designed for ESL learners with lower-level proficiency (SCoRE, 2021). We also draw parallels with the Newsela-Auto and Wiki-Auto datasets, which are used for text simplification and language learning (Newsela-Auto, 2020). By providing a dataset that focuses on the specific needs of ESL learners, SC-Ques can help bridge the gap between language models' performance and real-world language learning applications. Our goal is to facilitate the development of more effective language models that can support ESL learners in their language acquisition journey.
gold_abstract:
Sentence completion (SC) questions present a sentence with one or more blanks
that need to be filled in, three to five possible words or phrases as options.
SC questions are widely used for students learning English as a Second Language
(ESL). In this paper, we present a large-scale SC dataset, \textsc{SC-Ques},
which is made up of 292,517 ESL SC questions from real-world standardized
English examinations. Furthermore, we build a comprehensive benchmark of
automatically solving the SC questions by training the large-scale pre-trained
language models on the proposed \textsc{SC-Ques} dataset. We conduct detailed
analysis of the baseline models performance, limitations and trade-offs. The
data and our code are available for research purposes from:
\url{https://github.com/ai4ed/SC-Ques}.

title: ``It's how you do things that matters'': Attending to Process to Better Serve Indigenous Communities with Language Technologies
generated_abstract: This paper argues that the process of developing language technologies for indigenous communities matters as much as the technologies themselves, highlighting the importance of decolonization and community engagement in language documentation and technology development. Our work draws on the challenges of manual transcription and the limitations of automatic speech recognition in preserving endangered languages, such as Aboriginal languages in Australia, where recordings are often not transcribed, and efforts to build ASR systems are hindered by the lack of annotated data. This is reflected in the need for meaningful bonds with the speech community, as advocated by researchers working on languages like Karuk and Cayuga, and the importance of identifying real problems that can be solved by language technologies in minority communities. We also emphasize the need for a comprehensive approach to data collection, as proposed in best practices for data collection in rural and illiterate communities, to minimize bias and unethical practices. By prioritizing community-driven initiatives and collaboration, we can build language technologies that truly serve the needs of indigenous communities, rather than commodifying their languages as data.
gold_abstract: Indigenous languages are historically under-served by Natural Language Processing (NLP) technologies, but this is changing for some languages with the recent scaling of large multilingual models and an increased focus by the NLP community on endangered languages. This position paper explores ethical considerations in building NLP technologies for Indigenous languages, based on the premise that such projects should primarily serve Indigenous communities. We report on interviews with 17 researchers working in or with Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander communities on language technology projects in Australia. Drawing on insights from the interviews, we recommend practices for NLP researchers to increase attention to the process of engagements with Indigenous communities, rather than focusing only on decontextualised artefacts.

title:
Is It Worth the (Environmental) Cost? Limited Evidence for the Benefits
  of Diachronic Continuous Training
generated_abstract: Despite the undeniable argument that training safer language models (LMs) is costly, research on this topic lags behind research on training ever-larger LMs on raw data due to the difficulty of curating knowledge bases and decreased training speed. Finetuning, a less costly approach, may reduce the general-purpose utility of LMs and has not been shown to be effective in reducing information-related harms. The investigation of continual learning in the pretraining phase is an open direction for future research, as it may provide valuable insights into knowledge acquisition and retention. Our study focuses on the OLMo 1B and 7B models, which publicly provide intermediate pretraining checkpoints, and finds that performance tends to decline when resuscitating models at their initial or mid-stages. This suggests that the benefits of diachronic continuous training may not outweigh the environmental costs, which include the difficulty of curating knowledge bases and the decreased training speed.
gold_abstract:
Language is constantly changing and evolving, leaving language models to
quickly become outdated, both factually and linguistically. Recent research
proposes we continuously update our models using new data. Continuous training
allows us to teach language models about new events and facts and changing
norms. However, continuous training also means continuous costs. We show there
is currently limited evidence for the benefits of continuous training, be it
for the actual downstream performance or the environmental cost. Our results
show continuous training does not significantly improve performance. While it
is clear that, sooner or later, our language models need to be updated, it is
unclear when this effort is worth the cost. We call for a critical reflection
about when and how to use continuous training and for more benchmarks to
support this research direction.

title:
Interpretable Multi-Head Self-Attention model for Sarcasm Detection in
  social media
generated_abstract: This paper proposes an Interpretable Multi-Head Self-Attention model for Sarcasm Detection in social media, addressing the limitations of existing approaches in capturing complex relations between sarcastic utterances and their conversational context. Building on the graph attention-based model that jointly models users' social and historical context (2), we incorporate a multi-head self-attention mechanism to identify important sarcastic keywords and learn long-range dependencies between them (3). Our model achieves state-of-the-art results on the expanded SPIRS dataset (2). We also demonstrate the importance of exploiting relationships between a sarcastic tweet and its conversational context in modeling sarcastic utterances (2). In contrast to user-based models, detecting sarcastic intentions of the author is easier than identifying the sarcasm perceived by others (2).
gold_abstract:
Sarcasm is a linguistic expression often used to communicate the opposite of
what is said, usually something that is very unpleasant with an intention to
insult or ridicule. Inherent ambiguity in sarcastic expressions, make sarcasm
detection very difficult. In this work, we focus on detecting sarcasm in
textual conversations from various social networking platforms and online
media. To this end, we develop an interpretable deep learning model using
multi-head self-attention and gated recurrent units. Multi-head self-attention
module aids in identifying crucial sarcastic cue-words from the input, and the
recurrent units learn long-range dependencies between these cue-words to better
classify the input text. We show the effectiveness of our approach by achieving
state-of-the-art results on multiple datasets from social networking platforms
and online media. Models trained using our proposed approach are easily
interpretable and enable identifying sarcastic cues in the input text which
contribute to the final classification score. We visualize the learned
attention weights on few sample input texts to showcase the effectiveness and
interpretability of our model.

title: Evaluating Gender Bias in Machine Translation
generated_abstract: This paper evaluates gender bias in machine translation (MT) systems by introducing an extension of the WinoMT challenge set, WiBeMT, which assesses gender-bias in MT systems beyond occupations. Our work is grounded in prior research that has shown MT systems, such as DeepL, Microsoft, and Google Translate, inherit gender bias from their training data and word embeddings (Saunders et al., 2010). Recent studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of debiasing methods, including using gender annotations in training data and filtering source monolingual data by gender to create additional gender-specific training data (Saunders et al., 2010; 2104.07695). However, these approaches have shown mixed results, and there is still a need for more effective methods to mitigate gender bias in MT systems. Our work contributes to this effort by introducing a novel evaluation framework for assessing gender bias in MT systems, specifically for the Hindi language, which has received little attention in this area. By analyzing the performance of three MT systems, we aim to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the current state of gender bias in MT and identify potential areas for improvement.
gold_abstract:
We present the first challenge set and evaluation protocol for the analysis
of gender bias in machine translation (MT). Our approach uses two recent
coreference resolution datasets composed of English sentences which cast
participants into non-stereotypical gender roles (e.g., "The doctor asked the
nurse to help her in the operation"). We devise an automatic gender bias
evaluation method for eight target languages with grammatical gender, based on
morphological analysis (e.g., the use of female inflection for the word
"doctor"). Our analyses show that four popular industrial MT systems and two
recent state-of-the-art academic MT models are significantly prone to
gender-biased translation errors for all tested target languages. Our data and
code are made publicly available.

title: Toward Diverse Precondition Generation
generated_abstract: Toward Diverse Precondition Generation This paper addresses the challenge of generating diverse preconditions for natural language processing tasks, building upon the success of attribute-driven generation and diversity boosting strategies. Inspired by the BenchMaker method, which optimizes multiple attributes such as diversity, accuracy, and difficulty, we aim to enhance sample diversity and controllability for a given task description. Our approach leverages the diversity-augmented objective of Diverse Beam Search to produce a list of outputs that can serve as preconditions for downstream tasks. By adopting a similar framework to the Contrastive Learning paradigm, we can generate representations that are similar for positive examples and dissimilar for negative examples. Our goal is to improve the quality of preconditions for tasks such as machine translation and dialogue generation, where diverse and meaningful outputs are crucial.
gold_abstract:
Language understanding must identify the logical connections between events
in a discourse, but core events are often unstated due to their commonsense
nature. This paper fills in these missing events by generating precondition
events. Precondition generation can be framed as a sequence-to-sequence
problem: given a target event, generate a possible precondition. However, in
most real-world scenarios, an event can have several preconditions, requiring
diverse generation -- a challenge for standard seq2seq approaches. We propose
DiP, a Diverse Precondition generation system that can generate unique and
diverse preconditions. DiP uses a generative process with three components --
an event sampler, a candidate generator, and a post-processor. The event
sampler provides control codes (precondition triggers) which the candidate
generator uses to focus its generation. Unlike other conditional generation
systems, DiP automatically generates control codes without training on diverse
examples. Analysis against baselines reveals that DiP improves the diversity of
preconditions significantly while also generating more preconditions.

title: Context-Aware Language Modeling for Goal-Oriented Dialogue Systems
generated_abstract: Context-aware language modeling has emerged as a promising approach for goal-oriented dialogue systems, addressing the limitations of traditional task-oriented frameworks that rely on discrete intents and slots. By leveraging large language models (LLMs), our approach reduces the effort involved in framing intents and training classifiers, making the development of new task-oriented dialogues simpler and faster. In contrast to existing approaches that often require annotated data for building an intent model, our method can adapt to distributional shifts in user utterances, as demonstrated in the MultiWOZ dataset and the Microsoft Dialog Dataset. We draw inspiration from the successes of neural architectures on natural language processing subtasks, where LLMs have shown sample efficiency and flexibility. Our context-aware language model has the potential to improve the performance of dialogue systems in a variety of domains, including customer support and booking tasks.
gold_abstract:
Goal-oriented dialogue systems face a trade-off between fluent language
generation and task-specific control. While supervised learning with large
language models is capable of producing realistic text, how to steer such
responses towards completing a specific task without sacrificing language
quality remains an open question. In this work, we formulate goal-oriented
dialogue as a partially observed Markov decision process, interpreting the
language model as a representation of both the dynamics and the policy. This
view allows us to extend techniques from learning-based control, such as task
relabeling, to derive a simple and effective method to finetune language models
in a goal-aware way, leading to significantly improved task performance. We
additionally introduce a number of training strategies that serve to better
focus the model on the task at hand. We evaluate our method, Context-Aware
Language Models (CALM), on a practical flight-booking task using AirDialogue.
Empirically, CALM outperforms the state-of-the-art method by 7% in terms of
task success, matching human-level task performance.

title:
QUAK: A Synthetic Quality Estimation Dataset for Korean-English Neural
  Machine Translation
generated_abstract: This paper introduces QUAK, a comprehensive dataset for Korean-English neural machine translation quality estimation. QUAK addresses the limitations of existing Korean benchmark datasets, which may contain incorrect translations and grammatical errors, particularly when generated from English sources (Korean benchmark datasets). By leveraging in-house linguistic expertise, QUAK mitigates these issues and provides a highly grammatical and natural dataset for evaluating machine translation quality. Our dataset is designed to facilitate the development of quality estimation models that can handle multiple language pairs efficiently, addressing the challenges of resource-intensive training and large memory requirements. This work contributes to the advancement of machine translation quality estimation, particularly for low-resource languages, and provides a valuable resource for the research community.
gold_abstract:
With the recent advance in neural machine translation demonstrating its
importance, research on quality estimation (QE) has been steadily progressing.
QE aims to automatically predict the quality of machine translation (MT) output
without reference sentences. Despite its high utility in the real world, there
remain several limitations concerning manual QE data creation: inevitably
incurred non-trivial costs due to the need for translation experts, and issues
with data scaling and language expansion. To tackle these limitations, we
present QUAK, a Korean-English synthetic QE dataset generated in a fully
automatic manner. This consists of three sub-QUAK datasets QUAK-M, QUAK-P, and
QUAK-H, produced through three strategies that are relatively free from
language constraints. Since each strategy requires no human effort, which
facilitates scalability, we scale our data up to 1.58M for QUAK-P, H and 6.58M
for QUAK-M. As an experiment, we quantitatively analyze word-level QE results
in various ways while performing statistical analysis. Moreover, we show that
datasets scaled in an efficient way also contribute to performance improvements
by observing meaningful performance gains in QUAK-M, P when adding data up to
1.58M.

title: The Gaps between Pre-train and Downstream Settings in Bias Evaluation and Debiasing
generated_abstract: Our work investigates the discrepancies between pre-training and downstream settings in bias evaluation and debiasing. We examine the findings of prior research , which suggests that intrinsic biases in pre-trained language models (PLMs) can transfer to downstream tasks and that intrinsic debiasing can have a positive effect on downstream fairness. However, this is in contrast to , which found poor correlation between intrinsic and extrinsic biases. We propose a new framework to evaluate and mitigate bias in PLMs, considering techniques such as Counterfactual Data Augmentation (CDA) and Iterative Nullspace Projection (INLP). We demonstrate the utility of our framework using the BBQ dataset , which provides a more comprehensive evaluation of biases in language models. Our results show that biases can be effectively mitigated using these debiasing techniques, with promising implications for improving fairness in downstream tasks.
gold_abstract: The output tendencies of {PLM}s vary markedly before and after {FT} due to the updates to the model parameters. These divergences in output tendencies result in a gap in the social biases of {PLM}s. For example, there exits a low correlation between intrinsic bias scores of a {PLM} and its extrinsic bias scores under {FT}-based debiasing methods. Additionally, applying {FT}-based debiasing methods to a {PLM} leads to a decline in performance in downstream tasks. On the other hand, PLMs trained on large datasets can learn without parameter updates via {ICL} using prompts. {ICL} induces smaller changes to {PLM}s compared to {FT}-based debiasing methods. Therefore, we hypothesize that the gap observed in pre-trained and {FT} models does not hold true for debiasing methods that use {ICL}. In this study, we demonstrate that {ICL}-based debiasing methods show a higher correlation between intrinsic and extrinsic bias scores compared to {FT}-based methods. Moreover, the performance degradation due to debiasing is also lower in the {ICL} case compared to that in the {FT} case.

title:
Russian Language Datasets in the Digitial Humanities Domain and Their
  Evaluation with Word Embeddings
generated_abstract: This paper focuses on the evaluation of Russian language datasets in the digital humanities domain using word embeddings, a ubiquitous tool in natural language processing. Our research builds upon previous studies, such as the work on word2word dataset, a collection of bilingual lexicons for 3,564 language pairs, which provides word translations of high quality. We identify a gap in existing datasets, specifically in the English-NL to Russian-PL (programming language) domain, and aim to address this gap by creating a comprehensive collection of supervised datasets containing NL mapped onto PL snippets. The creation of these datasets is informed by the systematic survey of English NL-PL datasets, as described in. Our approach leverages the utility of word embeddings in mapping semantic and syntactic relationships among words, as demonstrated in. We also draw inspiration from the evaluation of Russian language resources, including the creation of a refined version of the Russian part of Multilingual SimLex999, as reported in.
gold_abstract:
In this paper, we present Russian language datasets in the digital humanities
domain for the evaluation of word embedding techniques or similar language
modeling and feature learning algorithms. The datasets are split into two task
types, word intrusion and word analogy, and contain 31362 task units in total.
The characteristics of the tasks and datasets are that they build upon small,
domain-specific corpora, and that the datasets contain a high number of named
entities. The datasets were created manually for two fantasy novel book series
("A Song of Ice and Fire" and "Harry Potter"). We provide baseline evaluations
with popular word embedding models trained on the book corpora for the given
tasks, both for the Russian and English language versions of the datasets.
Finally, we compare and analyze the results and discuss specifics of Russian
language with regards to the problem setting.

title: Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Opinion Target Extraction
generated_abstract: This paper introduces Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Opinion Target Extraction, a novel approach to sentiment analysis that bridges language gaps in opinion target extraction. By leveraging a pre-trained multilingual language model as a baseline, we explore the benefits of learning paragraph-level sentiment detection as an intermediate task, as demonstrated in the work of . We propose a novel approach to incorporate sentiment information into bilingual embeddings, which we extend to targeted cross-lingual sentiment classification, similar to the Bilingual Sentiment Embeddings (Blse) of . Our method is evaluated on diverse linguistic environments, including the XSUM dataset, where we achieve promising results in opinion target extraction and classification. We also draw upon the findings of on the few-shot performance of language models, informing our evaluation strategy.
gold_abstract:
Aspect-based sentiment analysis involves the recognition of so called opinion
target expressions (OTEs). To automatically extract OTEs, supervised learning
algorithms are usually employed which are trained on manually annotated
corpora. The creation of these corpora is labor-intensive and sufficiently
large datasets are therefore usually only available for a very narrow selection
of languages and domains. In this work, we address the lack of available
annotated data for specific languages by proposing a zero-shot cross-lingual
approach for the extraction of opinion target expressions. We leverage
multilingual word embeddings that share a common vector space across various
languages and incorporate these into a convolutional neural network
architecture for OTE extraction. Our experiments with 5 languages give
promising results: We can successfully train a model on annotated data of a
source language and perform accurate prediction on a target language without
ever using any annotated samples in that target language. Depending on the
source and target language pairs, we reach performances in a zero-shot regime
of up to 77% of a model trained on target language data. Furthermore, we can
increase this performance up to 87% of a baseline model trained on target
language data by performing cross-lingual learning from multiple source
languages.

title:
Alternate Endings: Improving Prosody for Incremental Neural TTS with
  Predicted Future Text Input
generated_abstract: This paper presents a novel approach to improving prosody in incremental neural text-to-speech synthesis (iTTS) by leveraging predicted future text input. Our method builds upon the success of text prompts in expressing speaking styles in natural language, while addressing the limitations of current systems in controlling prosody, emotion, and acoustic environment independently. Inspired by recent advancements in duration modelling, we develop an approach that predicts the probability of advancing to the next phone in the phonetic sequence at each acoustic frame, enabling the modelling of any duration distribution on the positive integers. This work is motivated by the importance of high-level, long-range dependencies in prosodic sequence modelling, as noted in the literature on recurrent models such as LSTMs. By predicting the future text input, we can better capture the complex, out-of-distribution sequence generation task of iTTS and generate more natural-sounding synthetic speech.
gold_abstract:
The prosody of a spoken word is determined by its surrounding context. In
incremental text-to-speech synthesis, where the synthesizer produces an output
before it has access to the complete input, the full context is often unknown
which can result in a loss of naturalness in the synthesized speech. In this
paper, we investigate whether the use of predicted future text can attenuate
this loss. We compare several test conditions of next future word: (a) unknown
(zero-word), (b) language model predicted, (c) randomly predicted and (d)
ground-truth. We measure the prosodic features (pitch, energy and duration) and
find that predicted text provides significant improvements over a zero-word
lookahead, but only slight gains over random-word lookahead. We confirm these
results with a perceptive test.

title:
gundapusunil at SemEval-2020 Task 9: Syntactic Semantic LSTM
  Architecture for SENTIment Analysis of Code-MIXed Data
generated_abstract: This paper presents our approach to sentiment analysis of Hindi-English code-mixed data, a challenging task due to the lack of labeled datasets and the noisy nature of the data. We draw inspiration from existing work on code-mixed sentiment analysis, where LSTM models have been shown to capture rich sequential patterns in English data but struggle with the limited and sparse code-mixed data. In contrast, ensemble models combining traditional and deep learning approaches have been successful in improving accuracy. Our proposed system, a syntactic-semantic LSTM architecture, is designed to address the specific challenges of code-mixed data and outperform traditional machine learning approaches. We evaluate our approach on a shared task dataset from ICON-2017 and demonstrate its effectiveness in sentiment analysis of code-mixed data.
gold_abstract:
The phenomenon of mixing the vocabulary and syntax of multiple languages
within the same utterance is called Code-Mixing. This is more evident in
multilingual societies. In this paper, we have developed a system for SemEval
2020: Task 9 on Sentiment Analysis for Code-Mixed Social Media Text. Our system
first generates two types of embeddings for the social media text. In those,
the first one is character level embeddings to encode the character level
information and to handle the out-of-vocabulary entries and the second one is
FastText word embeddings for capturing morphology and semantics. These two
embeddings were passed to the LSTM network and the system outperformed the
baseline model.

title:
Automated Quality Assessment of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy Sessions
  Through Highly Contextualized Language Representations
generated_abstract: This paper proposes an automated quality assessment mechanism for cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) sessions through highly contextualized language representations. By leveraging a sequence-to-sequence natural language generation model, trained on a small, anonymized psychotherapist-client text conversation dataset, we aim to ease the volume at human point-of-care and reduce stigma surrounding psychotherapeutic care. Recent progress in large language models has enabled automated inference of mental health scores from patient-generated natural language, but comprehensive evaluations indicate limitations in their accuracy and consistency. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel approach that incorporates mental health domain knowledge using the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) to assess depressive symptoms. Our experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach in evaluating CBT sessions, highlighting the importance of domain knowledge in improving accuracy and consistency.
gold_abstract:
During a psychotherapy session, the counselor typically adopts techniques
which are codified along specific dimensions (e.g., 'displays warmth and
confidence', or 'attempts to set up collaboration') to facilitate the
evaluation of the session. Those constructs, traditionally scored by trained
human raters, reflect the complex nature of psychotherapy and highly depend on
the context of the interaction. Recent advances in deep contextualized language
models offer an avenue for accurate in-domain linguistic representations which
can lead to robust recognition and scoring of such psychotherapy-relevant
behavioral constructs, and support quality assurance and supervision. In this
work, we propose a BERT-based model for automatic behavioral scoring of a
specific type of psychotherapy, called Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT),
where prior work is limited to frequency-based language features and/or short
text excerpts which do not capture the unique elements involved in a
spontaneous long conversational interaction. The model focuses on the
classification of therapy sessions with respect to the overall score achieved
on the widely-used Cognitive Therapy Rating Scale (CTRS), but is trained in a
multi-task manner in order to achieve higher interpretability. BERT-based
representations are further augmented with available therapy metadata,
providing relevant non-linguistic context and leading to consistent performance
improvements. We train and evaluate our models on a set of 1,118 real-world
therapy sessions, recorded and automatically transcribed. Our best model
achieves an F1 score equal to 72.61% on the binary classification task of low
vs. high total CTRS.

title: Focused Concatenation for Context-Aware Neural Machine Translation
generated_abstract: This work proposes Focused Concatenation, a novel approach to context-aware neural machine translation (NMT) that addresses the limitations of existing multi-encoder models by leveraging the effectiveness of single-encoder approaches. By integrating the ideas of multi-encoding and single-encoder methods, Focused Concatenation aims to bridge the gap between the two paradigms. In contrast to previous studies (, which employed conventional single language-pair settings, our approach focuses on incorporating multiple languages in the source, as seen in the increasing attention to low-resource languages (). By building upon the successes of single-encoder architectures (, we demonstrate the benefits of context discounting, which allows for efficient learning of contextual parameters. This approach has the potential to improve the performance of NMT models on tasks such as machine translation evaluation campaigns ().
gold_abstract:
A straightforward approach to context-aware neural machine translation
consists in feeding the standard encoder-decoder architecture with a window of
consecutive sentences, formed by the current sentence and a number of sentences
from its context concatenated to it. In this work, we propose an improved
concatenation approach that encourages the model to focus on the translation of
the current sentence, discounting the loss generated by target context. We also
propose an additional improvement that strengthen the notion of sentence
boundaries and of relative sentence distance, facilitating model compliance to
the context-discounted objective. We evaluate our approach with both
average-translation quality metrics and contrastive test sets for the
translation of inter-sentential discourse phenomena, proving its superiority to
the vanilla concatenation approach and other sophisticated context-aware
systems.

title:
Metric for Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation based on Universal
  Sentence Representations
generated_abstract: Automatic machine translation (MT) evaluation is crucial for assessing the performance of MT systems, yet current metrics have limitations, such as BLEU, which has little correlation with human judgment on the segment level . The evaluation of MT systems is a necessary task during research . Recent studies have proposed segment-level metrics, such as RUSE , which use pre-trained sentence embeddings to capture global information. However, human evaluators apply different standards when assessing machine translation output , making it challenging to obtain meaningful scores across language pairs. This paper proposes a new metric for automatic MT evaluation based on universal sentence representations, aiming to address these limitations and provide a more comprehensive evaluation of MT systems.
gold_abstract:
Sentence representations can capture a wide range of information that cannot
be captured by local features based on character or word N-grams. This paper
examines the usefulness of universal sentence representations for evaluating
the quality of machine translation. Although it is difficult to train sentence
representations using small-scale translation datasets with manual evaluation,
sentence representations trained from large-scale data in other tasks can
improve the automatic evaluation of machine translation. Experimental results
of the WMT-2016 dataset show that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art
performance with sentence representation features only.

title: CrudeOilNews: An Annotated Crude Oil News Corpus for Event Extraction
generated_abstract: We present CrudeOilNews, an annotated commodity news corpus for event extraction, comprising 425 documents, 7,059 sentences, and 10,578 events. This dataset addresses the need for a publicly available resource in the commodity news domain, where events are mainly Geo-political, Macro-economics, supply and demand in nature. CrudeOilNews is annotated with 18 event types, 21 entity types, and event metadata, including polarity, modality, and intensity. Our work aims to facilitate future research in this area, which has been challenging due to the limited amount of labeled data and class imbalance in event properties distribution. By leveraging transfer learning from a source domain with high similarity, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in addressing the unique challenges posed by this dataset. The proposed corpus and methodology can serve as a benchmark for event extraction tasks in the commodity news domain.
gold_abstract:
In this paper, we present CrudeOilNews, a corpus of English Crude Oil news
for event extraction. It is the first of its kind for Commodity News and serve
to contribute towards resource building for economic and financial text mining.
This paper describes the data collection process, the annotation methodology
and the event typology used in producing the corpus. Firstly, a seed set of 175
news articles were manually annotated, of which a subset of 25 news were used
as the adjudicated reference test set for inter-annotator and system
evaluation. Agreement was generally substantial and annotator performance was
adequate, indicating that the annotation scheme produces consistent event
annotations of high quality. Subsequently the dataset is expanded through (1)
data augmentation and (2) Human-in-the-loop active learning. The resulting
corpus has 425 news articles with approximately 11k events annotated. As part
of active learning process, the corpus was used to train basic event extraction
models for machine labeling, the resulting models also serve as a validation or
as a pilot study demonstrating the use of the corpus in machine learning
purposes. The annotated corpus is made available for academic research purpose
at https://github.com/meisin/CrudeOilNews-Corpus.

title:
Improving Low-Resource Speech Recognition with Pretrained Speech Models:
  Continued Pretraining vs. Semi-Supervised Training
generated_abstract: We present a study on improving low-resource speech recognition using pre-trained speech models, comparing continued pretraining and semi-supervised training (Section 1.1). Our findings are grounded in the work of prior studies that utilized semi-supervised learning to refine acoustic models with unlabeled data (1). In low-resource settings, where large labeled datasets are scarce, semi-supervised learning offers a promising approach to improve speech recognition performance (2). However, recent work has also shown the effectiveness of multilingual pretraining for speech recognition (3), suggesting that pretraining can be a powerful tool for improving low-resource languages. We investigate the trade-offs between continued pretraining and semi-supervised training, and our results suggest that semi-supervised training can achieve comparable performance to fully supervised training with a significantly reduced need for labeled data (1).
gold_abstract:
Self-supervised Transformer based models, such as wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT,
have produced significant improvements over existing approaches to automatic
speech recognition (ASR). This is evident in the performance of the wav2vec 2.0
based pretrained XLSR-53 model across many languages when fine-tuned with
available labeled data. However, the performance from finetuning these models
can be dependent on the amount of in-language or similar-to-in-language data
included in the pretraining dataset. In this paper we investigate continued
pretraining (CoPT) with unlabeled in-language audio data on the XLSR-53
pretrained model in several low-resource languages. CoPT is more
computationally efficient than semi-supervised training (SST), the standard
approach of utilizing unlabeled data in ASR, since it omits the need for
pseudo-labeling of the unlabeled data. We show CoPT results in word error rates
(WERs), equal to or slightly better than using SST. In addition, we show that
using the CoPT model for pseudo-labeling, and using these labels in SST,
results in further improvements in WER.

title: arXiv 2505.20977
generated_abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in industrial applications, specifically designed to assess their capabilities and limitations in real-world scenarios. Our benchmark, MME-Industry, features a diverse set of evaluation questions across 21 different industries, providing a thorough assessment of MLLMs' performance. In contrast to their impressive capabilities on complex tasks, MLLMs significantly underperform humans on trivial tasks, as highlighted in the LMentry benchmark. We demonstrate the importance of understanding the underlying principles governing MLLMs' behavior, as emphasized by the taxonomy of language model evaluation proposed in the work on LMentry. Our evaluation framework builds upon the successes of recent MLLMs, such as Gemini-1.5Pro and Qwen2-VL, which have advanced complex reasoning and understanding through scaled architectures and extensive datasets.
gold_abstract: Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success on complex multi-modal tasks. However, it remains insufficiently explored whether they exhibit modality preference, a tendency to favor one modality over another when processing multi-modal contexts. To study this question, we introduce {MC {2}} benchmark, which constructs controlled evidence-conflict scenarios to systematically evaluate modality preference in decision-making. Extensive experiments reveal that all 20 tested MLLMs generally demonstrate clear modality preferences, and such preferences can serve as a useful indicator of downstream task performance of MLLMs. Further analysis shows that modality preference can be controlled by instruction guidance and captured within the latent representations of MLLMs. Built on these insights, we propose a probing and steering method based on representation engineering to explicitly control modality preference without requiring additional fine-tuning. This method effectively amplifies modality preference toward a desired direction and demonstrates promising improvements across multiple multi-modal understanding and reasoning tasks. { }{} {The code and data are released at {https://github.com/EchoDreamer/Modality-Preference}.} {footnote}{-1}

title:
Compositional Generalisation with Structured Reordering and Fertility
  Layers
generated_abstract: This paper introduces an approach to compositional generalization with structured reordering and fertility layers, aiming to improve compositional generalization in neural language models. Building on the literature that customizes neural architectures to promote lexical generalization via explicit disentanglement of token meanings (e.g., CGPS, SyntAtt), we propose a novel method that leverages structured attention and marginal permutations to align words or substructures in input sequences with their counterparts in output sequences. Empirical evidence from existing studies (e.g.,,) highlights the importance of assessing compositional generalization in natural language models. Our approach is distinct from existing methods that utilize data augmentation or learned networks to transform out-of-distribution compositional generalization tasks into in-distribution ones (e.g.,). By exploring the relationships between discrete algorithmic ideas, classical combinatorial optimization, and modern deep learning, our method demonstrates a promising direction for operationalizing compositional generalization in neural language models.
gold_abstract:
Seq2seq models have been shown to struggle with compositional generalisation,
i.e. generalising to new and potentially more complex structures than seen
during training. Taking inspiration from grammar-based models that excel at
compositional generalisation, we present a flexible end-to-end differentiable
neural model that composes two structural operations: a fertility step, which
we introduce in this work, and a reordering step based on previous work (Wang
et al., 2021). Our model outperforms seq2seq models by a wide margin on
challenging compositional splits of realistic semantic parsing tasks that
require generalisation to longer examples. It also compares favourably to other
models targeting compositional generalisation.

title: Generating Demonstrations for In-Context Compositional Generalization in Grounded Language Learning
generated_abstract: We propose a novel approach to generating demonstrations for in-context compositional generalization in grounded language learning, leveraging the recent advancements in in-context learning (ICL) paradigm (Brown et al., 2020; Dong, 2024). ICL has been shown to be effective in unlocking the advanced capabilities of large language models and has become a standard technique for tackling various tasks (Peng et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2025). However, the choice of demonstrations significantly impacts the final performance of LLMs, and designing the selection process based on criteria such as complexity and diversity can enhance model performance (Fu et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023). Our approach focuses on few-shot ICL, where demonstrations are selected from the available pool and attached to the real query input to form the prompt for the LLM.
