We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a real-world compositional generalization challenge (Pan et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2023).
gold_abstract: In-Context-learning and few-shot prompting are viable methods compositional output generation. However, these methods can be very sensitive to the choice of support examples used. Retrieving good supports from the training data for a given test query is already a difficult problem, but in some cases solving this may not even be enough. We consider the setting of grounded language learning problems where finding relevant supports in the same or similar states as the query may be difficult. We design an agent which instead generates possible supports inputs and targets current state of the world, then uses them in-context-learning to solve the test query. We show substantially improved performance on a previously unsolved compositional generalization test without a loss of performance in other areas. The approach is general and can even scale to instructions expressed in natural language.

title:
ConveRT: Efficient and Accurate Conversational Representations from
  Transformers
generated_abstract: ConveRT: Efficient and Accurate Conversational Representations from Transformers ConveRT, a lightweight transformer-based dual-encoder network, has been proposed to learn pre-trained representations for conversational AI applications, particularly for real-world Intent Classification tasks (Conversational Representations from Transformers). This architecture leverages quantization and sub-word level parameterization, and its pre-trained representation outputs can be utilized for Intent Classification tasks. When incorporating ConveRT embeddings within the DIET classifier, the initial embeddings for the classification tasks are improved. In contrast, existing methods, such as the embedding augmentation method for dialogue models, which combines training pairs of samples and labels convexly, focus on enhancing the diversity within a single sample (Dialogue Embedding Augmentation). This paper explores the application of ConveRT for conversational AI tasks, aiming to provide efficient and accurate representations from transformers.
gold_abstract:
General-purpose pretrained sentence encoders such as BERT are not ideal for
real-world conversational AI applications; they are computationally heavy,
slow, and expensive to train. We propose ConveRT (Conversational
Representations from Transformers), a pretraining framework for conversational
tasks satisfying all the following requirements: it is effective, affordable,
and quick to train. We pretrain using a retrieval-based response selection
task, effectively leveraging quantization and subword-level parameterization in
the dual encoder to build a lightweight memory- and energy-efficient model. We
show that ConveRT achieves state-of-the-art performance across widely
established response selection tasks. We also demonstrate that the use of
extended dialog history as context yields further performance gains. Finally,
we show that pretrained representations from the proposed encoder can be
transferred to the intent classification task, yielding strong results across
three diverse data sets. ConveRT trains substantially faster than standard
sentence encoders or previous state-of-the-art dual encoders. With its reduced
size and superior performance, we believe this model promises wider portability
and scalability for Conversational AI applications.

title: textless-lib: a Library for Textless Spoken Language Processing
generated_abstract: Textless-lib, a novel library for textless spoken language processing, aims to bridge the gap in speech and language technology for under-resourced languages lacking textual resources. Building upon the idea that infants can learn their native language through observation and interaction, we propose a framework that enables the development of textless AI applications, as demonstrated in the Zero Resource Speech Challenge Series (ZR15,17,19,20). Our approach focuses on the discovery of subword units, a key component in speech processing, which has been successfully applied in various speech-to-text and text-to-speech systems. By leveraging the strengths of Speech Language Models (SpeechLMs), we can bypass the need for transcription or automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems, as shown in and. Our library is designed to be applicable for any language in the world, given the universal nature of small, discrete phonetic inventories.
gold_abstract:
Textless spoken language processing research aims to extend the applicability
of standard NLP toolset onto spoken language and languages with few or no
textual resources. In this paper, we introduce textless-lib, a PyTorch-based
library aimed to facilitate research in this research area. We describe the
building blocks that the library provides and demonstrate its usability by
discuss three different use-case examples: (i) speaker probing, (ii) speech
resynthesis and compression, and (iii) speech continuation. We believe that
textless-lib substantially simplifies research the textless setting and will be
handful not only for speech researchers but also for the NLP community at
large. The code, documentation, and pre-trained models are available at
https://github.com/facebookresearch/textlesslib/ .

title:
Synonym Knowledge Enhanced Reader for Chinese Idiom Reading
  Comprehension
generated_abstract: This paper proposes a novel Chinese idiom paraphrasing (CIP) task, which aims to rephrase idiom-included sentences to non-idiom-included ones, differing from existing paraphrase generation research. To address this task, we construct a large-scale training dataset for CIP through a three-stage pipeline that leverages both machine translation methods and human annotators. The dataset is designed to meet two requirements: (1) the two sentences in a sentence pair convey the same meaning, and (2) each sentence pair contains an idiom-included sentence and its paraphrase. Our proposed CIP methods, Infill and Knowledge, are evaluated on this dataset, yielding good results.
gold_abstract:
Machine reading comprehension (MRC) is the task that asks a machine to answer
questions based on a given context. For Chinese MRC, due to the non-literal and
non-compositional semantic characteristics, Chinese idioms pose unique
challenges for machines to understand. Previous studies tend to treat idioms
separately without fully exploiting the relationship among them. In this paper,
we first define the concept of literal meaning coverage to measure the
consistency between semantics and literal meanings for Chinese idioms. With the
definition, we prove that the literal meanings of many idioms are far from
their semantics, and we also verify that the synonymic relationship can
mitigate this inconsistency, which would be beneficial for idiom comprehension.
Furthermore, to fully utilize the synonymic relationship, we propose the
synonym knowledge enhanced reader. Specifically, for each idiom, we first
construct a synonym graph according to the annotations from a high-quality
synonym dictionary or the cosine similarity between the pre-trained idiom
embeddings and then incorporate the graph attention network and gate mechanism
to encode the graph. Experimental results on ChID, a large-scale Chinese idiom
reading comprehension dataset, show that our model achieves state-of-the-art
performance.

title:
Adaptive Activation Network For Low Resource Multilingual Speech
  Recognition
generated_abstract: This paper presents Adaptive Activation Network for Low Resource Multilingual Speech Recognition, which addresses the challenge of training deep learning based acoustic models for low-resource languages. The availability of large speech and language resources for training deep learning models is a limiting factor for many languages, as emphasized in, where large training corpus is required for the acoustic models. Cross-lingual transfer, as discussed in, is an attractive solution, but recent advances in Self Supervised Learning are opening up avenues for unlabeled speech data. Our proposed Adaptive Activation Network aims to leverage the benefits of cross-lingual transfer and self-supervised learning, while also addressing the computational efficiency of weight factorization, as demonstrated in. We draw inspiration from recent advances in multilingual ASR, such as the use of language-id signals and language-specific parameters, as seen in.
gold_abstract:
Low resource automatic speech recognition (ASR) is a useful but thorny task,
since deep learning ASR models usually need huge amounts of training data. The
existing models mostly established a bottleneck (BN) layer by pre-training on a
large source language, and transferring to the low resource target language. In
this work, we introduced an adaptive activation network to the upper layers of
ASR model, and applied different activation functions to different languages.
We also proposed two approaches to train the model: (1) cross-lingual learning,
replacing the activation function from source language to target language, (2)
multilingual learning, jointly training the Connectionist Temporal
Classification (CTC) loss of each language and the relevance of different
languages. Our experiments on IARPA Babel datasets demonstrated that our
approaches outperform the from-scratch training and traditional bottleneck
feature based methods. In addition, combining the cross-lingual learning and
multilingual learning together could further improve the performance of
multilingual speech recognition.

title:
Dyn-ASR: Compact, Multilingual Speech Recognition via Spoken Language
  and Accent Identification
generated_abstract: We present Dyn-ASR, a compact, multilingual speech recognition system via spoken language and accent identification. Our approach is grounded in the findings of, which demonstrated the effectiveness of fine-tuning a pre-trained model on a small amount of labeled data to improve non-native speech recognition. Building upon this, we explore the benefits of using a language model in decoding ASR outputs, as observed in, and the importance of adequate labeled data for multilingual tasks, as highlighted in. Our approach is also informed by recent advancements in self-supervised cross-lingual speech representation models, such as XLS-R, which scaled the number of languages and the amount of training data. In contrast to, which proposed a novel architecture for multi-task training on ASR, our system focuses on the specific task of accent identification. We evaluate our approach on a range of datasets, including's held-out ASR dataset and's CommonVoice dataset.
gold_abstract:
Running automatic speech recognition (ASR) on edge devices is non-trivial due
to resource constraints, especially in scenarios that require supporting
multiple languages. We propose a new approach to enable multilingual speech
recognition on edge devices. This approach uses both language identification
and accent identification to select one of multiple monolingual ASR models
on-the-fly, each fine-tuned for a particular accent. Initial results for both
recognition performance and resource usage are promising with our approach
using less than 1/12th of the memory consumed by other solutions.

title: LScDC-new large scientific dictionary
generated_abstract: This paper presents LScDC-new, a large scientific dictionary built from the Leicester Scientific Corpus (LSC), a newly constructed dataset of 1.2 million scientific abstracts from 2000 to 2020. LSC is used to quantify meaning in academic texts, particularly in the context of lexical semantic change detection (LSCD), a task that has seen considerable developments in recent years, with type-based embeddings dominating in diachronic scenarios. We compare our dictionary with the New Academic Word List (NAWL), a reference resource for second language learners of English. Our approach is based on the LIWC-22 dictionary, which is used for text analysis, and we evaluate the usefulness of our semantic change discovery procedure from a lexicographer's viewpoint. This work contributes to the field of LSCD by providing a large-scale, scientifically-annotated corpus and a dictionary that can be used to analyze the evolution of word meanings in scientific texts.
gold_abstract:
In this paper, we present a scientific corpus of abstracts of academic papers
in English -- Leicester Scientific Corpus (LSC). The LSC contains 1,673,824
abstracts of research articles and proceeding papers indexed by Web of Science
(WoS) in which publication year is 2014. Each abstract is assigned to at least
one of 252 subject categories. Paper metadata include these categories and the
number of citations. We then develop scientific dictionaries named Leicester
Scientific Dictionary (LScD) and Leicester Scientific Dictionary-Core (LScDC),
where words are extracted from the LSC. The LScD is a list of 974,238 unique
words (lemmas). The LScDC is a core list (sub-list) of the LScD with 104,223
lemmas. It was created by removing LScD words appearing in not greater than 10
texts in the LSC. LScD and LScDC are available online. Both the corpus and
dictionaries are developed to be later used for quantification of meaning in
academic texts.
  Finally, the core list LScDC was analysed by comparing its words and word
frequencies with a classic academic word list 'New Academic Word List (NAWL)'
containing 963 word families, which is also sampled from an academic corpus.
The major sources of the corpus where NAWL is extracted are Cambridge English
Corpus (CEC), oral sources and textbooks. We investigate whether two
dictionaries are similar in terms of common words and ranking of words. Our
comparison leads us to main conclusion: most of words of NAWL (99.6%) are
present in the LScDC but two lists differ in word ranking. This difference is
measured.

title: DeepHate: Hate Speech Detection via Multi-Faceted Text Representations
generated_abstract: This paper presents DeepHate, a novel approach to hate speech detection via multi-faceted text representations. Building on the importance of diverse, annotated datasets for training effective predictive models (Maity et al., 2024), DeepHate leverages a systematic annotation process to incorporate sentiment analysis. The proposed model employs a dual-channel architecture, where sentiment representation is used in conjunction with semantic representation to classify hate speech. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach in detecting hate speech across languages, underscoring the need for high-quality datasets in hate speech detection. In contrast to implicit hate detection, which has gained attention recently (Eb656d432fb43b1d92c8dc240afb2ed5f5800914), DeepHate focuses on explicit hate speech detection. By adapting to new resources and social media comments, DeepHate serves as a robust means to build more effective hate-speech detection models.
gold_abstract:
Online hate speech is an important issue that breaks the cohesiveness of
online social communities and even raises public safety concerns in our
societies. Motivated by this rising issue, researchers have developed many
traditional machine learning and deep learning methods to detect hate speech in
online social platforms automatically. However, most of these methods have only
considered single type textual feature, e.g., term frequency, or using word
embeddings. Such approaches neglect the other rich textual information that
could be utilized to improve hate speech detection. In this paper, we propose
DeepHate, a novel deep learning model that combines multi-faceted text
representations such as word embeddings, sentiments, and topical information,
to detect hate speech in online social platforms. We conduct extensive
experiments and evaluate DeepHate on three large publicly available real-world
datasets. Our experiment results show that DeepHate outperforms the
state-of-the-art baselines on the hate speech detection task. We also perform
case studies to provide insights into the salient features that best aid in
detecting hate speech in online social platforms.

title: VSEC: Transformer-based Model for Vietnamese Spelling Correction
generated_abstract: This paper presents VSEC, a transformer-based model for Vietnamese spelling correction, which shares similarities with the Chinese Spelling Correction (CSC) task. Like SCOPE, VSEC utilizes phonetic knowledge in conjunction with a transformer model for spelling correction. In contrast to the CSC task, where spelling errors are often intentional, our task focuses on correcting misspellings in user-generated short text, such as queries in search engines or tweets on social media. We fine-tune our method with a standard classifier of BERT Masked LM for spelling correction. The proposed approach is inspired by SoftMask and FASPell, which employ BERT with soft-mask generators and sequence-to-sequence predictions, respectively.
gold_abstract:
Spelling error correction is one of topics which have a long history in
natural language processing. Although previous studies have achieved remarkable
results, challenges still exist. In the Vietnamese language, a state-of-the-art
method for the task infers a syllable's context from its adjacent syllables.
The method's accuracy can be unsatisfactory, however, because the model may
lose the context if two (or more) spelling mistakes stand near each other. In
this paper, we propose a novel method to correct Vietnamese spelling errors. We
tackle the problems of mistyped errors and misspelled errors by using a deep
learning model. The embedding layer, in particular, is powered by the byte pair
encoding technique. The sequence to sequence model based on the Transformer
architecture makes our approach different from the previous works on the same
problem. In the experiment, we train the model with a large synthetic dataset,
which is randomly introduced spelling errors. We test the performance of the
proposed method using a realistic dataset. This dataset contains 11,202
human-made misspellings in 9,341 different Vietnamese sentences. The
experimental results show that our method achieves encouraging performance with
86.8% errors detected and 81.5% errors corrected, which improves the
state-of-the-art approach 5.6% and 2.2%, respectively.

title: Identifying and Categorizing Offensive Language in Social Media
generated_abstract: This paper presents an emoji-based method for collecting and categorizing offensive and hate speech in social media, demonstrating its effectiveness in collecting a higher percentage of offensive content compared to existing methods. Building on the work of previous studies, such as the OffensEval task and the dataset of 67fbc49ab4b47128d15fad1d7b34e5de8501a85d, which focuses on identifying offensive language in tweets, our approach relies on lexical features and a dictionary-based blacklist approach. The proposed method is shown to be effective in collecting large numbers of offensive contents in languages like Bangla. We also draw from the annotation process of 2103.14972, which manually annotated a corpus of offensive language on social media, and the OffenEval task 67fbc49ab4b47128d15fad1d7b34e5de8501a85d. Our findings highlight the importance of developing automated detection models specifically designed for identifying offensive language, as emphasized in 2308.10592, to mitigate online abuse.
gold_abstract:
Offensive language is pervasive in social media. Individuals frequently take
advantage of the perceived anonymity of computer-mediated communication, using
this to engage in behavior that many of them would not consider in real life.
The automatic identification of offensive content online is an important task
that has gained more attention in recent years. This task can be modeled as a
supervised classification problem in which systems are trained using a dataset
containing posts that are annotated with respect to the presence of some
form(s) of abusive or offensive content. The objective of this study is to
provide a description of a classification system built for SemEval-2019 Task 6:
OffensEval. This system classifies a tweet as either offensive or not offensive
(Sub-task A) and further classifies offensive tweets into categories (Sub-tasks
B \& C). We trained machine learning and deep learning models along with data
preprocessing and sampling techniques to come up with the best results. Models
discussed include Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest and
LSTM.

title: Are Mutually Intelligible Languages Easier to Translate?
generated_abstract: This paper investigates whether mutually intelligible languages are easier to translate. We draw on research on language similarity and its relation to translation quality, as discussed in, which shows that linguistic similarities can facilitate successful oral intercomprehension among closely-related languages. However, the evaluation of machine translation systems has traditionally focused on automatic metrics, such as those used in, which presents a comprehensive dataset for machine translation, and, which highlights the limitations of automatic evaluation metrics. In contrast, demonstrates that human interpreters perform better than machine translation systems in terms of intelligibility, suggesting that a user-centric approach may be more suitable for evaluating translation quality. We propose a novel approach to assess the performance of machine translation systems in a communicative setting, leveraging the findings from and, which compare machine translation with human interpretation. Our goal is to determine whether languages with high mutual intelligibility are easier to translate using machine translation systems.
gold_abstract:
Two languages are considered mutually intelligible if their native speakers
can communicate with each other, while using their own mother tongue. How does
the fact that humans perceive a language pair as mutually intelligible affect
the ability to learn a translation model between them? We hypothesize that the
amount of data needed to train a neural ma-chine translation model is
anti-proportional to the languages' mutual intelligibility. Experiments on the
Romance language group reveal that there is indeed strong correlation between
the area under a model's learning curve and mutual intelligibility scores
obtained by studying human speakers.

title: On the Language Coverage Bias for Neural Machine Translation
generated_abstract: This paper explores the language coverage bias in neural machine translation, where the performance of NMT models varies significantly across languages, especially for low-resource languages. Our work is motivated by the observation that NMT has historically focused on high-resource languages (e.g.,) and the scarcity of high-quality training data across diverse languages. We investigate how language coverage bias affects the performance of NMT models and propose a comprehensive dataset and benchmark to explore realistic low-resource scenarios and zero-shot machine translation. Our findings suggest that language coverage bias is a significant issue in NMT, and we demonstrate the importance of developing models that can handle diverse languages and language variants. We also highlight the need for more robust and adaptable NMT models that can mitigate the bias and improve performance on low-resource languages.
gold_abstract:
Language coverage bias, which indicates the content-dependent differences
between sentence pairs originating from the source and target languages, is
important for neural machine translation (NMT) because the target-original
training data is not well exploited in current practice. By carefully designing
experiments, we provide comprehensive analyses of the language coverage bias in
the training data, and find that using only the source-original data achieves
comparable performance with using full training data. Based on these
observations, we further propose two simple and effective approaches to
alleviate the language coverage bias problem through explicitly distinguishing
between the source- and target-original training data, which consistently
improve the performance over strong baselines on six WMT20 translation tasks.
Complementary to the translationese effect, language coverage bias provides
another explanation for the performance drop caused by back-translation. We
also apply our approach to both back- and forward-translation and find that
mitigating the language coverage bias can improve the performance of both the
two representative data augmentation methods and their tagged variants.

title: Multi-Task Pre-Training for Plug-and-Play Task-Oriented Dialogue System
generated_abstract: We present a multi-task pre-training approach for task-oriented dialogue systems, leveraging existing data in high-resource languages to reduce the laborious process of building new datasets for low-resource languages. Our approach draws from the success of pre-trained models in dialogue generation, such as DialoGPT, which adapts a GPT-2 model on large-scale conversation datasets. In this work, we pre-train a model on supplementary data to improve its ability to perform various dialogue tasks, including domain classification and dialogue generation. By utilizing a text-to-text framework, our model can be fine-tuned for specific tasks, reducing the need for rule-based grammars or large labeled datasets. Our approach builds upon the development of encoder-decoder frameworks, which have been found promising for both chit-chat and task-oriented dialogues. By leveraging pre-trained language models and the flexibility of canonical form schemas, we aim to simplify and speed up the development of new task-oriented dialogues.
gold_abstract:
Pre-trained language models have been recently shown to benefit task-oriented
dialogue (TOD) systems. Despite their success, existing methods often formulate
this task as a cascaded generation problem which can lead to error accumulation
across different sub-tasks and greater data annotation overhead. In this study,
we present PPTOD, a unified plug-and-play model for task-oriented dialogue. In
addition, we introduce a new dialogue multi-task pre-training strategy that
allows the model to learn the primary TOD task completion skills from
heterogeneous dialog corpora. We extensively test our model on three benchmark
TOD tasks, including end-to-end dialogue modelling, dialogue state tracking,
and intent classification. Experimental results show that PPTOD achieves new
state of the art on all evaluated tasks in both high-resource and low-resource
scenarios. Furthermore, comparisons against previous SOTA methods show that the
responses generated by PPTOD are more factually correct and semantically
coherent as judged by human annotators.

title:
Neural News Recommendation with Collaborative News Encoding and
  Structural User Encoding
generated_abstract: We present a novel approach to neural news recommendation, dubbed Neural News Recommendation with Collaborative News Encoding and Structural User Encoding. Our work builds upon the paradigm of projecting text data into a vector space, as observed in, to effectively model text-rich news content. Unlike existing works on news modeling, which primarily focus on clustering and classification, our approach utilizes a novel approach to mine web-scale knowledge, demonstrating strong performance on clustering benchmarks. We evaluate our model on the MIND dataset, a large-scale public news recommendation dataset, and show its generalization ability in a news retrieval task using the NewsBERT model, as in and. Our findings indicate that directly fine-tuning the generally pre-trained UniLM model is worse than using the models distilled in the news recommendation task, as observed in. We compare our approach with strong baselines, including DAN, QA-LSTM, Attentive-BiLSTM, and ESIM, as in.
gold_abstract:
Automatic news recommendation has gained much attention from the academic
community and industry. Recent studies reveal that the key to this task lies
within the effective representation learning of both news and users. Existing
works typically encode news title and content separately while neglecting their
semantic interaction, which is inadequate for news text comprehension. Besides,
previous models encode user browsing history without leveraging the structural
correlation of user browsed news to reflect user interests explicitly. In this
work, we propose a news recommendation framework consisting of collaborative
news encoding (CNE) and structural user encoding (SUE) to enhance news and user
representation learning. CNE equipped with bidirectional LSTMs encodes news
title and content collaboratively with cross-selection and cross-attention
modules to learn semantic-interactive news representations. SUE utilizes graph
convolutional networks to extract cluster-structural features of user history,
followed by intra-cluster and inter-cluster attention modules to learn
hierarchical user interest representations. Experiment results on the MIND
dataset validate the effectiveness of our model to improve the performance of
news recommendation. Our code is released at
https://github.com/Veason-silverbullet/NNR.

title: FLEX: A Benchmark for Evaluating Robustness of Fairness\ Large Language Models
generated_abstract: This paper introduces FLEX, a benchmark for evaluating the robustness of fairness in large language models under extreme scenarios. Building on the idea of comprehensive coverage from AdvGLUE, FLEX aims to cover various adversarial linguistic phenomena, including word-level transformations and human-written adversarial examples. FLEX evaluates the performance of language models on tasks that involve fairness, such as identifying biases shared across different models, as highlighted in the work that challenged and unveiled the biases shared across different models. The paper also cites the importance of systematic annotations, as stated in the work that performs systematic and comprehensive evaluation and annotation of robustness failures in NLP models. By evaluating the robustness of fairness in large language models, FLEX provides a diagnostic benchmark for researchers to develop more robust models.
gold_abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly enhanced interactions between users and models. These advancements concurrently underscore the need for rigorous safety evaluations due to the manifestation of social biases, which can lead to harmful societal impacts. Despite these concerns, existing benchmarks may overlook the intrinsic weaknesses of LLMs, which can generate biased responses even with simple adversarial instructions. To address this critical gap, we introduce a new benchmark, Fairness Benchmark in LLM under Extreme Scenarios (FLEX), designed to test whether LLMs can sustain fairness even when exposed to prompts constructed to induce bias. To thoroughly evaluate the robustness of LLMs, we integrate prompts that amplify potential biases into the fairness assessment. Comparative experiments between FLEX and existing benchmarks demonstrate that traditional evaluations may underestimate the inherent risks in models. This highlights the need for more stringent LLM evaluation benchmarks to guarantee safety and fairness. Our data and code are available at {https://github.com/ekgus9/FLEX}.

title:
Augmenting Compositional Models for Knowledge Base Completion Using
  Gradient Representations
generated_abstract: Augmenting Compositional Models for Knowledge Base Completion Using Gradient Representations We propose novel KB completion models that leverage structural and semantic context from graph convolutional networks (GCNs) and language model representations to address the sparsity and scale challenges in commonsense knowledge graphs. Our method effectively utilizes information from both sources through a progressive masking strategy. By incorporating semantic similarity edges, we demonstrate that BERT-based node representations can significantly improve performance, especially when the text in the graph is similar to pre-training corpora. Furthermore, graph embeddings provide rich local context for encoding nodes and boost performance when trained with subgraph structures.
gold_abstract:
Neural models of Knowledge Base data have typically employed compositional
representations of graph objects: entity and relation embeddings are
systematically combined to evaluate the truth of a candidate Knowedge Base
entry. Using a model inspired by Harmonic Grammar, we propose to tokenize
triplet embeddings by subjecting them to a process of optimization with respect
to learned well-formedness conditions on Knowledge Base triplets. The resulting
model, known as Gradient Graphs, leads to sizable improvements when implemented
as a companion to compositional models. Also, we show that the
"supracompositional" triplet token embeddings it produces have interpretable
properties that prove helpful in performing inference on the resulting triplet
representations.

title: Muppet: Massive Multi-task Representations with Pre-Finetuning
generated_abstract: We present Muppet, a novel approach to multi-task learning that leverages pre-finetuning to capture task and language interactions in multi-task, multi-lingual settings. By drawing from recent works on meta-learning, such as the use of XTREME multilingual benchmark dataset, our method demonstrates clear performance improvements over competitive baseline models, particularly for deeper tasks. In contrast to existing approaches that require access to a large amount of labeled data from unrelated tasks, Muppet focuses on utilizing human-annotated training datasets to learn how to generate synthetic samples of a given task. Our experiments on a set of selected tasks, filtered from a broader set, show that Muppet is effective in deriving task embeddings and improving finetuning performance. The results suggest that Muppet is a promising method for efficiently using multitask data, particularly when a single finetuning task data is available.
gold_abstract:
We propose pre-finetuning, an additional large-scale learning stage between
language model pre-training and fine-tuning. Pre-finetuning is massively
multi-task learning (around 50 datasets, over 4.8 million total labeled
examples), and is designed to encourage learning of representations that
generalize better to many different tasks. We show that pre-finetuning
consistently improves performance for pretrained discriminators (e.g.~RoBERTa)
and generation models (e.g.~BART) on a wide range of tasks (sentence
prediction, commonsense reasoning, MRC, etc.), while also significantly
improving sample efficiency during fine-tuning. We also show that large-scale
multi-tasking is crucial; pre-finetuning can hurt performance when few tasks
are used up until a critical point (usually above 15) after which performance
improves linearly in the number of tasks.

title: Prompting Is Programming: A Query Language For Large Language Models
generated_abstract: We propose a novel approach to programming large language models via a query language, which we refer to as \ours (\textbf{H}ybrid \textbf{Pro}gram-Based \textbf{Pro}mpting) for Hybrid Question Answering (HQA) tasks. Inspired by recent studies that leverage prompts to guide zero-shot generation without fine-tuning, our framework considers the solution as a process of code generation and execution, integrating external customized functions under the few-shot setting. In contrast to finetuning-based methods, our prompting-based approach maintains the ability of a single large language model to perform many tasks. By using available natural language descriptions of schema categories associated with database documentation as task-specific promptings for DST, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Our proposed method is the first to use a prompting-based approach to show that reasoning can be elicited in off-the-shelf language models of sufficient scale, without modifying the model.
gold_abstract:
Large language models have demonstrated outstanding performance on a wide
range of tasks such as question answering and code generation. On a high level,
given an input, a language model can be used to automatically complete the
sequence in a statistically-likely way. Based on this, users prompt these
models with language instructions or examples, to implement a variety of
downstream tasks. Advanced prompting methods can even imply interaction between
the language model, a user, and external tools such as calculators. However, to
obtain state-of-the-art performance or adapt language models for specific
tasks, complex task- and model-specific programs have to be implemented, which
may still require ad-hoc interaction.
  Based on this, we present the novel idea of Language Model Programming (LMP).
LMP generalizes language model prompting from pure text prompts to an intuitive
combination of text prompting and scripting. Additionally, LMP allows
constraints to be specified over the language model output. This enables easy
adaption to many tasks, while abstracting language model internals and
providing high-level semantics. To enable LMP, we implement LMQL (short for
Language Model Query Language), which leverages the constraints and control
flow from an LMP prompt to generate an efficient inference procedure that
minimizes the number of expensive calls to the underlying language model. We
show that LMQL can capture a wide range of state-of-the-art prompting methods
in an intuitive way, especially facilitating interactive flows that are
challenging to implement with existing high-level APIs. Our evaluation shows
that we retain or increase the accuracy on several downstream tasks, while also
significantly reducing the required amount of computation or cost in the case
of pay-to-use APIs (13-85% cost savings).

title: An Automatic Evaluation of the WMT22 General Machine Translation Task
generated_abstract: This paper presents an automatic evaluation of the WMT22 general machine translation task, focusing on ranking with metrics and statistical significance testing. The evaluation of machine translation systems without human intervention has gained importance, as seen in the improvement of BLEU e4769c780f4d648b03975db9b0206d03b0c30d01 in the last decade. However, BLEU has little correlation with human judgment on the segment level. We propose a novel approach combining naive MBR and oracle combinations to evaluate MT outputs, addressing the issue of segment-level evaluation. Our evaluation also considers MT hypotheses that are semantically equivalent with different surfaces from the reference, as in METEOR-Universal.
gold_abstract:
This report presents an automatic evaluation of the general machine
translation task of the Seventh Conference on Machine Translation (WMT22). It
evaluates a total of 185 systems for 21 translation directions including
high-resource to low-resource language pairs and from closely related to
distant languages. This large-scale automatic evaluation highlights some of the
current limits of state-of-the-art machine translation systems. It also shows
how automatic metrics, namely chrF, BLEU, and COMET, can complement themselves
to mitigate their own limits in terms of interpretability and accuracy.

title: FREDA: Flexible Relation Extraction Data Annotation
generated_abstract: This work proposes FREDA, a novel framework for flexible relation extraction data annotation. Building upon the limitations of traditional supervised approaches, which require manual labor to create hand-crafted extraction patterns or patterns learned from hand-labeled training examples, FREDA aims to alleviate the need for extensive annotation efforts. As highlighted in the TACRED and DocRED datasets, traditional approaches to relation extraction often focus on identifying and extracting relations of interest, but may not scale well to new domains (SpERT,). In contrast, FREDA enables the efficient annotation of relations across multiple attributes, avoiding the structural constraints of binary or event schema. The proposed framework has the potential to simplify approaches for extracting relations with an arbitrary number of attributes, as demonstrated by the effectiveness of MARE models in extracting relations without candidate enumeration and trigger concepts (MARE,). By leveraging the flexibility of FREDA, relation extraction can be made more efficient and scalable, with applications in various domains, including information extraction and natural language processing.
gold_abstract:
To effectively train accurate Relation Extraction models, sufficient and
properly labeled data is required. Adequately labeled data is difficult to
obtain and annotating such data is a tricky undertaking. Previous works have
shown that either accuracy has to be sacrificed or the task is extremely
time-consuming, if done accurately. We are proposing an approach in order to
produce high-quality datasets for the task of Relation Extraction quickly.
Neural models, trained to do Relation Extraction on the created datasets,
achieve very good results and generalize well to other datasets. In our study,
we were able to annotate 10,022 sentences for 19 relations in a reasonable
amount of time, and trained a commonly used baseline model for each relation.

title:
No Intruder, no Validity: Evaluation Criteria for Privacy-Preserving
  Text Anonymization
generated_abstract: This work investigates the challenges of evaluating text anonymization techniques for machine learning models. Evaluating the effectiveness of anonymization methods is crucial to ensure the validity of the results, yet existing methods often rely on technical performance metrics alone (Section 2 of). In contrast, we argue that a comprehensive evaluation should consider both the anonymization success and the characteristics of the output text (Section 2 of). To address this gap, we present a structured analysis of text anonymization and its impact on machine learning model performance, using seven different anonymization techniques on multiple clinical NLP tasks (Section 1 of). Our findings highlight the importance of considering the specific decision-making process and the particular requirements of each task.
gold_abstract:
For sensitive text data to be shared among NLP researchers and practitioners,
shared documents need to comply with data protection and privacy laws. There is
hence a growing interest in automated approaches for text anonymization.
However, measuring such methods' performance is challenging: missing a single
identifying attribute can reveal an individual's identity. In this paper, we
draw attention to this problem and argue that researchers and practitioners
developing automated text anonymization systems should carefully assess whether
their evaluation methods truly reflect the system's ability to protect
individuals from being re-identified. We then propose TILD, a set of evaluation
criteria that comprises an anonymization method's technical performance, the
information loss resulting from its anonymization, and the human ability to
de-anonymize redacted documents. These criteria may facilitate progress towards
a standardized way for measuring anonymization performance.

title: Cross-Lingual Phrase Retrieval
generated_abstract: This paper investigates cross-lingual phrase retrieval, a crucial component of cross-lingual information retrieval (CLIR). We focus on the scenario where some relevance judgments are available and can be leveraged as supervision for fine-tuning multilingual encoders for ad-hoc retrieval (2112.11031). Specifically, we consider two common scenarios: (1) an abundance of relevance annotations from other retrieval tasks and collections, and (2) a small number of relevance judgments for the target collection. In the latter case, we use a small number of CLEF relevance judgments to fine-tune the representation space of multilingual encoders via contrastive metric-based learning. Our approach is informed by previous studies that demonstrate the effectiveness of contrastive learning in improving cross-lingual representations (e.g., 2205.08605, 2209.02071, 2105.02472). By leveraging contrastive learning and fine-tuning multilingual encoders, we aim to improve the performance of cross-lingual phrase retrieval and bridge the lexical gap between languages.
gold_abstract:
Cross-lingual retrieval aims to retrieve relevant text across languages.
Current methods typically achieve cross-lingual retrieval by learning
language-agnostic text representations in word or sentence level. However, how
to learn phrase representations for cross-lingual phrase retrieval is still an
open problem. In this paper, we propose XPR, a cross-lingual phrase retriever
that extracts phrase representations from unlabeled example sentences.
Moreover, we create a large-scale cross-lingual phrase retrieval dataset, which
contains 65K bilingual phrase pairs and 4.2M example sentences in 8
English-centric language pairs. Experimental results show that XPR outperforms
state-of-the-art baselines which utilize word-level or sentence-level
representations. XPR also shows impressive zero-shot transferability that
enables the model to perform retrieval in an unseen language pair during
training. Our dataset, code, and trained models are publicly available at
www.github.com/cwszz/XPR/.

title: ``Seeing the Big through the Small'': Can LLMs Approximate Human Judgment Distributions on NLI from a Few Explanations?
generated_abstract: This paper explores the capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to approximate human judgment distributions on Natural Language Inference (NLI), specifically by examining the impact of incorporating explanations on their performance. Our study draws on prior work in LLMs' limitations in probabilistic reasoning , highlighting the need to investigate their ability to align with structured inference objectives . By combining the predictive power of LLMs with structured inference, we demonstrate consistent gains in performance over prompting alone, particularly when structuring the prompt as a true/false question and using the generated token probability as a confidence estimate . Our findings suggest that calibrating LLM scores based on structured prediction objectives can further boost performance, a result that holds true for both pre-trained and fine-tuned models . This research aims to provide insights into the potential of LLMs to approximate human judgment distributions in NLI tasks.
gold_abstract: Human label variation (HLV) is a valuable source of information that arises when multiple human annotators provide different labels for valid reasons. In Natural Language Inference (NLI) earlier approaches to capturing HLV involve either collecting annotations from many crowd workers to represent human judgment distribution (HJD) or use expert linguists to provide detailed explanations for their chosen labels. While the former method provides denser HJD information, obtaining it is resource-intensive. In contrast, the latter offers richer textual information but it is challenging to scale up to many human judges. Besides, large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as evaluators (``LLM judges'') but with mixed results, and few works aim to study HJDs. This study proposes to exploit LLMs to approximate HJDs using a small number of expert labels and explanations. Our experiments show that a few explanations significantly improve LLMs' ability to approximate HJDs with and without explicit labels, thereby providing a solution to scale up annotations for HJD. However, fine-tuning smaller soft-label aware models with the LLM-generated model judgment distributions (MJDs) presents partially inconsistent results: while similar in distance, their resulting fine-tuned models and visualized distributions differ substantially. We show the importance of complementing instance-level distance measures with a global-level shape metric and visualization to evaluate MJDs more effectively against human judgment distributions.

title: Long Short-Term Memory for Japanese Word Segmentation
generated_abstract: This paper explores the application of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to Japanese word segmentation, a fundamental task in natural language processing for Japanese. Inspired by the success of character-word LSTMs in English and Dutch, as demonstrated in, we investigate the effectiveness of this approach for Japanese. By incorporating character-level features, as shown in, our model leverages the structural information of words to improve representation and segmentation. We utilize character embeddings, similar to, to capture rich character-level information. The results demonstrate the potential of our approach in improving Japanese word segmentation, suggesting that it is a promising direction for future research in this area.
gold_abstract:
This study presents a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network approach
to Japanese word segmentation (JWS). Previous studies on Chinese word
segmentation (CWS) succeeded in using recurrent neural networks such as LSTM
and gated recurrent units (GRU). However, in contrast to Chinese, Japanese
includes several character types, such as hiragana, katakana, and kanji, that
produce orthographic variations and increase the difficulty of word
segmentation. Additionally, it is important for JWS tasks to consider a global
context, and yet traditional JWS approaches rely on local features. In order to
address this problem, this study proposes employing an LSTM-based approach to
JWS. The experimental results indicate that the proposed model achieves
state-of-the-art accuracy with respect to various Japanese corpora.

title: Cracking the Code of Hallucination in LVLMs with Vision-aware Head Divergence
generated_abstract: This paper, "Cracking the Code of Hallucination in LVLMs with Vision-aware Head Divergence," focuses on mitigating visual hallucinations in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), a critical challenge in their applications. Despite advancements in multimodal fusion, existing strategies for LLMs have been adapted, including iterative self-training frameworks and self-reflection mechanisms, yet benchmarks like ChartQA highlight challenges in complex visual and logical reasoning (). We draw inspiration from analyses focused on unimodal LLMs, which suggest that MLLMs may focus more on visual tokens in early layers and exhibit a better ability to perceive visual information in preceding layers compared to the final layers (, ). Our work aims to address the underlying causes of hallucinations in MLLMs, including factors such as excessive model depth and aggregation patterns, which are positively correlated with hallucinations and tend to emerge at deeper layers (). By exploring vision-aware head divergence, we aim to improve the reliability and trustworthiness of MLLMs in real-world applications.
gold_abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have made substantial progress in integrating large language models (LLMs) with visual inputs, enabling advanced multimodal reasoning. Despite their success, a persistent challenge is hallucination—where generated text fails to accurately reflect visual content—undermining both accuracy and reliability. Existing methods focus on alignment training or decoding refinements but primarily address symptoms at the generation stage without probing the underlying causes. In this work, we investigate the internal mechanisms driving hallucination in LVLMs, with an emphasis on the multi-head attention module. Specifically, we introduce Vision-aware Head Divergence (VHD), a metric that quantifies the sensitivity of attention head outputs to visual context. Based on this, our findings reveal the presence of vision-aware attention heads that are more attuned to visual information; however, the model's overreliance on its prior language patterns is closely related to hallucinations. Building on these insights, we propose Vision-aware Head Reinforcement (VHR), a training-free approach to mitigate hallucination by enhancing the role of vision-aware attention heads. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches in mitigating hallucinations, while maintaining high efficiency with negligible additional time overhead. The code is available at {https://github.com/jinghan1he/VHR}.

title: Learning Crosslingual Word Embeddings without Bilingual Corpora
generated_abstract: This paper proposes a novel approach to learning crosslingual word embeddings without relying on bilingual corpora. Our work is motivated by the lack of publicly available bilingual lexicons across diverse languages, which is highlighted by the existence of the word2word dataset, a collection of bilingual lexicons for 3,564 language pairs extracted from OpenSubtitles2018 (Mikolov et al., 2013; Faruqui and Dyer, 2014). Unlike existing methods that use machine translation to provide a training signal, our approach leverages the information from monolingual word embeddings, similar to the framework presented in LNMap (paper=2004.13889 section=Conclusions score=0.294). Our method is independently devised and more efficient than contrastive pretraining (paper=2105.02472 section=Contrastive Pretraining score=0.294), which uses the [CLS] representations of two BERT encoders. We aim to provide a simplified and task-specific solution for zero-shot cross-lingual models, which is also a focus of (DA,TS).
gold_abstract:
Crosslingual word embeddings represent lexical items from different languages
in the same vector space, enabling transfer of NLP tools. However, previous
attempts had expensive resource requirements, difficulty incorporating
monolingual data or were unable to handle polysemy. We address these drawbacks
in our method which takes advantage of a high coverage dictionary in an EM
style training algorithm over monolingual corpora in two languages. Our model
achieves state-of-the-art performance on bilingual lexicon induction task
exceeding models using large bilingual corpora, and competitive results on the
monolingual word similarity and cross-lingual document classification task.

title:
Thermostat: A Large Collection of NLP Model Explanations and Analysis
  Tools
generated_abstract: Thermostat: A Large Collection of NLP Model Explanations and Analysis Tools. This work presents Thermostat, a novel framework for unifying and evaluating NLP model explanations, leveraging the ERASER benchmark and ERASER's human-annotated rationales for model evaluation. By integrating with the Hugging Face Hub, Thermostat facilitates the introduction of post-hoc explainability techniques into existing NLP pipelines. A comprehensive evaluation of model explanations is provided, enabling users to select systematically from the vast design space of language model evaluation, and to measure 7 metrics, including accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, and efficiency. This work aims to facilitate progress on explainable models for NLP by providing a unified framework for generating, evaluating, and comparing explanations.
gold_abstract:
In the language domain, as in other domains, neural explainability takes an
ever more important role, with feature attribution methods on the forefront.
Many such methods require considerable computational resources and expert
knowledge about implementation details and parameter choices. To facilitate
research, we present Thermostat which consists of a large collection of model
explanations and accompanying analysis tools. Thermostat allows easy access to
over 200k explanations for the decisions of prominent state-of-the-art models
spanning across different NLP tasks, generated with multiple explainers. The
dataset took over 10k GPU hours (> one year) to compile; compute time that the
community now saves. The accompanying software tools allow to analyse
explanations instance-wise but also accumulatively on corpus level. Users can
investigate and compare models, datasets and explainers without the need to
orchestrate implementation details. Thermostat is fully open source,
democratizes explainability research in the language domain, circumvents
redundant computations and increases comparability and replicability.

title:
Demoting the Lead Bias in News Summarization via Alternating Adversarial
  Learning
generated_abstract: This paper proposes a unified model-agnostic debiasing strategy to mitigate distinct biases in news summarization while maintaining or strengthening the model's generalization power. Our approach is motivated by the observation that dataset-specific or model-specific debiasing methods may hinder performance on cross-datasets or cross-domain settings. We design a hierarchical multi-task learning framework to neutralize the framing bias in news titles, which serves as an indicator of the framing bias in the corresponding article. The proposed framework combines a summarization component and a position prediction component, and employs alternating adversarial learning to encourage the model to learn unbiased representations. Our approach is distinct from existing methods that focus on removing a subset of training data or perturbing the model's embedding space. By leveraging a debiasing loss function, our approach aims to discourage biased learning and improve the robustness of the target model.
gold_abstract:
In news articles the lead bias is a common phenomenon that usually dominates
the learning signals for neural extractive summarizers, severely limiting their
performance on data with different or even no bias. In this paper, we introduce
a novel technique to demote lead bias and make the summarizer focus more on the
content semantics. Experiments on two news corpora with different degrees of
lead bias show that our method can effectively demote the model's learned lead
bias and improve its generality on out-of-distribution data, with little to no
performance loss on in-distribution data.

title:
DESYR: Definition and Syntactic Representation Based Claim Detection on
  the Web
generated_abstract: This paper presents DESYR, a novel approach to claim detection on the web that leverages definition and syntactic representation. Our methodology, based on Dependency-Poincaré Embedding (DARÉ), addresses the task as a sequence labeling problem, where we extract a check-worthy main claim from user-generated content before fact-checking. In contrast to existing approaches that focus on political, statistical, and visual claims, our method is specifically designed to handle the complex structure and vocabulary of scientific claims. Our approach is grounded in the observation that fact-checking systems do not transfer robustly to colloquial claims, as shown in . By utilizing contextualized embeddings and fine-tuning with domain-specific knowledge, our method demonstrates a promising direction for claim detection in scientific contexts.
gold_abstract:
The formulation of a claim rests at the core of argument mining. To demarcate
between a claim and a non-claim is arduous for both humans and machines, owing
to latent linguistic variance between the two and the inadequacy of extensive
definition-based formalization. Furthermore, the increase in the usage of
online social media has resulted in an explosion of unsolicited information on
the web presented as informal text. To account for the aforementioned, in this
paper, we proposed DESYR. It is a framework that intends on annulling the said
issues for informal web-based text by leveraging a combination of hierarchical
representation learning (dependency-inspired Poincare embedding),
definition-based alignment, and feature projection. We do away with fine-tuning
computer-heavy language models in favor of fabricating a more domain-centric
but lighter approach. Experimental results indicate that DESYR builds upon the
state-of-the-art system across four benchmark claim datasets, most of which
were constructed with informal texts. We see an increase of 3 claim-F1 points
on the LESA-Twitter dataset, an increase of 1 claim-F1 point and 9 macro-F1
points on the Online Comments(OC) dataset, an increase of 24 claim-F1 points
and 17 macro-F1 points on the Web Discourse(WD) dataset, and an increase of 8
claim-F1 points and 5 macro-F1 points on the Micro Texts(MT) dataset. We also
perform an extensive analysis of the results. We make a 100-D pre-trained
version of our Poincare-variant along with the source code.

title: How Far Are We from Real Synonym Substitution Attacks?
generated_abstract: Synonym substitution attacks aim to manipulate language models by replacing words with their synonyms while preserving semantic meaning. However, recent studies have revealed that these attacks often result in grammatical errors and human perceptibility. To address this issue, we need to evaluate the distance between original and replaced words, as well as the semantic similarity between the original sentence and the one after perturbation. The current word-level adversarial attack research faces challenges in maintaining human prediction consistency, semantic similarity, and language fluency. As a result, researchers have employed various methods, including WordNet and distributional semantics, to improve lexical substitution and semantic relations.
gold_abstract:
In this paper, we explore the following question: how far are we from real
synonym substitution attacks (SSAs). We approach this question by examining how
SSAs replace words in the original sentence and show that there are still
unresolved obstacles that make current SSAs generate invalid adversarial
samples. We reveal that four widely used word substitution methods generate a
large fraction of invalid substitution words that are ungrammatical or do not
preserve the original sentence's semantics. Next, we show that the semantic and
grammatical constraints used in SSAs for detecting invalid word replacements
are highly insufficient in detecting invalid adversarial samples. Our work is
an important stepping stone to constructing better SSAs in the future.

title:
Task-adaptive Pre-training of Language Models with Word Embedding
  Regularization
generated_abstract: We propose a novel approach to pre-training language models with word embedding regularization, leveraging the concept of task-adaptive pre-training. Building on the success of traditional pre-training paradigms (Paradigm 1: Pre-Train then Fine-Tune,), we draw inspiration from the efficient and effective task-specific alignment of sentence embeddings generated by pre-trained language models (ULMFiT,). In contrast to previous approaches that rely on machine translation for cross-lingual transfer (DA,TS), we introduce a simplified and independently devised method for task-adaptive pre-training. Our approach is informed by the idea of distilling knowledge in large language models into embedding models (KaLM-Embedding,), and we utilize a pre-trained monolingual fastText model (fastText,) as a foundation for our embedding regularization. We aim to improve the performance of downstream NLP tasks by pre-training a language model on a broad general-domain corpus and fine-tuning it on the target task.
gold_abstract:
Pre-trained language models (PTLMs) acquire domain-independent linguistic
knowledge through pre-training with massive textual resources. Additional
pre-training is effective in adapting PTLMs to domains that are not well
covered by the pre-training corpora. Here, we focus on the static word
embeddings of PTLMs for domain adaptation to teach PTLMs domain-specific
meanings of words. We propose a novel fine-tuning process: task-adaptive
pre-training with word embedding regularization (TAPTER). TAPTER runs
additional pre-training by making the static word embeddings of a PTLM close to
the word embeddings obtained in the target domain with fastText. TAPTER
requires no additional corpus except for the training data of the downstream
task. We confirmed that TAPTER improves the performance of the standard
fine-tuning and the task-adaptive pre-training on BioASQ (question answering in
the biomedical domain) and on SQuAD (the Wikipedia domain) when their
pre-training corpora were not dominated by in-domain data.

title: Phylogeny-Inspired Adaptation of Multilingual Models to New Languages
generated_abstract: This paper presents a phylogeny-inspired adaptation approach to fine-tune a pretrained multilingual model for NMT using language-family adapters, as proposed in. Our approach leverages the modularity of adapters with effective cross-lingual transfer between related languages, outperforming both language-agnostic and language-pair adapters. This method groups languages together based on language families, contrasting with an automatic way of clustering data from different languages using a pretrained multilingual model. As observed in, large-scale multilingual language models often reach sub-optimal performance for languages with limited pretraining data. Our approach is particularly useful for languages new to mBART-50, as demonstrated by the experiments on 16 typologically diverse unseen languages in. By adapting a multilingual model to a new set of languages, as explored in, our method can be used to get usable language model-based representations for under-studied low-resource languages.
gold_abstract:
Large pretrained multilingual models, trained on dozens of languages, have
delivered promising results due to cross-lingual learning capabilities on
variety of language tasks. Further adapting these models to specific languages,
especially ones unseen during pre-training, is an important goal towards
expanding the coverage of language technologies. In this study, we show how we
can use language phylogenetic information to improve cross-lingual transfer
leveraging closely related languages in a structured, linguistically-informed
manner. We perform adapter-based training on languages from diverse language
families (Germanic, Uralic, Tupian, Uto-Aztecan) and evaluate on both syntactic
and semantic tasks, obtaining more than 20% relative performance improvements
over strong commonly used baselines, especially on languages unseen during
pre-training.

title: Decoding Emergent Big Five Traits in Large Language Models: Temperature-Dependent Expression and Architectural Clustering
generated_abstract: This paper explores the emergent big five traits in large language models, a long-standing pursuit in the field of human-like understanding and communication. Inspired by the big five personality model, we examine whether language models exhibit human-like patterns in thinking, feeling, and behaving, and if so, how to induce a specific personality in language models. We propose the mpi for evaluation, a zero-shot multiple-choice question-answering dataset, to verify the existence of personality in language models. Our analysis reveals that large language models exhibit temperature-dependent expression of personality traits, with certain traits being more pronounced at higher temperatures. This study contributes to the understanding of language models' personality and provides a foundation for future research in this area.
gold_abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) become integral to human-centered applications, understanding their personality-like behaviors is increasingly important for responsible development and deployment. This paper systematically evaluates six LLMs, applying the Big Five Inventory-2 (BFI-2) framework, to assess trait expressions under varying sampling temperatures. We find significant differences across four of the five personality dimensions, with Neuroticism and Extraversion susceptible to temperature adjustments. Further, hierarchical clustering reveals distinct model clusters, suggesting that architectural features may predispose certain models toward stable trait profiles. Taken together, these results offer new insights into the emergence of personality-like patterns in LLMs and provide a new perspective on model tuning, selection, and the ethical governance of AI systems. We share the data and code for this analysis here: {https://osf.io/bsvzc/?view_only=6672219bede24b4e875097426dc3fac1}

title: Explain with Visual Keypoints Like a Real Mentor!\ Benchmark for Multimodal Solution Explanation
generated_abstract: We present a benchmark for multimodal solution explanation, leveraging the comprehensive WikiHow knowledge base, which comprises over 100k articles with multimodal contents. Our benchmark focuses on procedural understanding, where each article describes a procedure to accomplish a desired task, paired with images and/or gifs. We draw inspiration from prior work that considers WikiHow for learning event temporal ordering, but extend it to a comprehensive multimodal understanding. In contrast to figure captioning and figure understanding research, which have evolved in isolation, our approach emphasizes the importance of visual faithfulness in multimodal explanation. By leveraging captions to bridge visual and textual modalities, we demonstrate strong performance on the SeePhys benchmark, similar to state-of-the-art methods that leverage captions for cross-modal alignment.
gold_abstract: With the rapid advancement of mathematical reasoning capabilities in Large Language Models (LLMs), AI systems are increasingly being adopted in educational settings to support students' comprehension of problem-solving processes. However, a critical component remains underexplored in current LLM-generated explanations: multimodal explanation. In real-world instructional contexts, human tutors routinely employ visual aids, such as diagrams, markings, and highlights, to enhance conceptual clarity. To bridge this gap, we introduce the task, designed to evaluate whether models can identify visual keypoints, such as auxiliary lines, points, angles, and generate explanations that incorporate these key elements essential for understanding. To evaluate model performance on this task, we propose , a multimodal benchmark consisting of 1,000 math problems annotated with visual keypoints and corresponding explanatory text that references those elements. Our empirical results show that current models struggle to identify visual keypoints. In the task of generating keypoint-based explanations, open-source models also face notable difficulties. This highlights a significant gap in current LLMs' ability to perform mathematical visual grounding, engage in visually grounded reasoning, and provide explanations in educational contexts. We expect that the task and the dataset will catalyze further research on LLMs in education and promote their use as effective, explanation-oriented AI tutors.

title: ChartMind: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Complex Real-world Multimodal Chart Question Answering
generated_abstract: We present ChartMind, a comprehensive benchmark for complex real-world multimodal chart question answering. Our dataset, comprising 9,608 human-written questions, addresses the limitations of existing chart question answering datasets that primarily rely on template-based questions and synthetic images (Section 1.1). We construct a robust evaluation framework by integrating the chart question answering task with multimodal QA and generation tasks, including ChartQA and Chart-to-Text summarization, to advance the field of visual language understanding (Section 1.2). In contrast to end-to-end solutions that require fine-tuning on large task datasets and still lag behind on complex queries (Section 3.2), ChartMind provides a platform for training and evaluating conversational question answering interfaces quantitatively, leveraging advanced deep learning models to improve the understanding of compositional and visual reasoning questions (Section 4.2). By exploring the intersection of information visualization and AI, ChartMind facilitates the development of more interpretable and cogent reasoning models, as emphasized in existing research (Section 1.1).
gold_abstract: Chart question answering (CQA) has become a critical multimodal task for evaluating the reasoning capabilities of vision-language models. While early approaches have shown promising performance by focusing on visual features or leveraging large-scale pre-training, most existing evaluations rely on rigid output formats and objective metrics, thus ignoring the complex, real-world demands of practical chart analysis. In this paper, we introduce ChartMind, a new benchmark designed for complex CQA tasks in real-world settings. ChartMind covers seven task categories, incorporates multilingual contexts, supports open-domain textual outputs, and accommodates diverse chart formats, bridging the gap between real-world applications and traditional academic benchmarks. Furthermore, we propose a context-aware yet model-agnostic framework, ChartLLM, that focuses on extracting key contextual elements, reducing noise, and enhancing the reasoning accuracy of multimodal large language models. Extensive evaluations on ChartMind and three representative public benchmarks with 14 mainstream multimodal models show our framework significantly outperforms the previous three common CQA paradigms: instruction-following, OCR-enhanced, and chain-of-thought, highlighting the importance of flexible chart understanding for real-world CQA. These findings suggest new directions for developing more robust chart reasoning in future research.

title: Annotation Guidelines for the Turku Paraphrase Corpus
generated_abstract: This paper presents the Turku Paraphrase Corpus, a manually annotated paraphrase corpus for Finnish, comprising 53,572 paraphrase pairs gathered from alternative movie or TV episode subtitles and news headings, with 7,909 hand-made rewrites turning context-dependent paraphrases into perfect paraphrases (Contributions outline: Label 4). The corpus is one of the largest manually annotated paraphrase datasets for any language, with 98% of the pairs manually classified as paraphrases in their given context. We also demonstrate the feasibility of manual paraphrase candidate selection from two parallel but monolingual documents, showing the technique to be feasible for high-quality candidate selection (Contributions outline: Label 3). Our work addresses the bias towards short, lexically overlapping paraphrases in existing corpora and aims to cater to the needs of Finnish NLP (Contributions outline: Label 2). The annotation process took 14 person-months, resulting in a corpus with longer, more lexically dissimilar paraphrases (Contributions outline: Label 1).
gold_abstract:
This document describes the annotation guidelines used to construct the Turku
Paraphrase Corpus. These guidelines were developed together with the corpus
annotation, revising and extending the guidelines regularly during the
annotation work. Our paraphrase annotation scheme uses the base scale 1-4,
where labels 1 and 2 are used for negative candidates (not paraphrases), while
labels 3 and 4 are paraphrases at least in the given context if not everywhere.
In addition to base labeling, the scheme is enriched with additional
subcategories (flags) for categorizing different types of paraphrases inside
the two positive labels, making the annotation scheme suitable for more
fine-grained paraphrase categorization. The annotation scheme is used to
annotate over 100,000 Finnish paraphrase pairs.

title: Word-Embeddings Distinguish Denominal and Root-Derived Verbs in Semitic
generated_abstract: This study investigates the capacity of word embeddings to distinguish denominal and root-derived verbs in Semitic languages, which exhibit complex morphology. Building on earlier work showing that word embeddings capture morphological properties in Hebrew , we leverage the two-level model, a framework that models both root and pattern morphology, to examine denominal verbs in Hebrew. The analysis reveals that standard training of word embeddings in Hebrew captures morphological properties, including the distinction between denominal and root-derived verbs, and that using the lemmas when composing the representations helps to better capture semantic similarities. In contrast to word-level morphological tasks, which have been extensively studied, clause-level morpho-syntactic modeling has received limited attention, highlighting the need for further research in this area. Our study contributes to the understanding of the utility of word embeddings in capturing complex morphological relationships in Semitic languages.
gold_abstract:
Proponents of the Distributed Morphology framework have posited the existence
of two levels of morphological word formation: a lower one, leading to loose
input-output semantic relationships; and an upper one, leading to tight
input-output semantic relationships. In this work, we propose to test the
validity of this assumption in the context of Hebrew word embeddings. If the
two-level hypothesis is borne out, we expect state-of-the-art Hebrew word
embeddings to encode (1) a noun, (2) a denominal derived from it (via an
upper-level operation), and (3) a verb related to the noun (via a lower-level
operation on the noun's root), in such a way that the denominal (2) should be
closer in the embedding space to the noun (1) than the related verb (3) is to
the same noun (1). We report that this hypothesis is verified by four embedding
models of Hebrew: fastText, GloVe, Word2Vec and AlephBERT. This suggests that
word embedding models are able to capture complex and fine-grained semantic
properties that are morphologically motivated.

title: Fine-grained prediction of food insecurity using news streams
generated_abstract: This paper presents a fine-grained prediction of food insecurity using news streams, focusing on the immigration portion of the Media Frames Corpus, which comprises 5,933 annotated articles. Building upon the GDELT database, which collects and classifies news articles worldwide, we develop a methodology that leverages natural language processing techniques to extract relevant information. Our approach involves frame-semantic parsing, keyword expansion, and dimensionality reduction to identify key aspects of food insecurity. By applying these techniques, we aim to provide valuable insights for real-time decision-making in areas such as disaster preparedness and humanitarian aid. We anticipate applications in extracting fine-grained policy proposals from party manifestos and identifying specific armed actors engaged in civil war violence.
gold_abstract:
Anticipating the outbreak of a food crisis is crucial to efficiently allocate
emergency relief and reduce human suffering. However, existing food insecurity
early warning systems rely on risk measures that are often delayed, outdated,
or incomplete. Here, we leverage recent advances in deep learning to extract
high-frequency precursors to food crises from the text of a large corpus of
news articles about fragile states published between 1980 and 2020. Our text
features are causally grounded, interpretable, validated by existing data, and
allow us to predict 32% more food crises than existing models up to three
months ahead of time at the district level across 15 fragile states. These
results could have profound implications on how humanitarian aid gets allocated
and open new avenues for machine learning to improve decision making in
data-scarce environments.

title:
Shared task: Lexical semantic change detection in German (Student
  Project Report)
