generated_abstract: This paper presents the results of our participation in the DIACR-Ita shared task on Lexical Semantic Change Detection in German, a subtask of the SemEval-2020 Unsupervised Lexical Semantic Change Detection shared task. Our approach is based on one of the earliest and most established semantic change detection models, utilizing Skip-Gram with Negative Sampling and Orthogonal Procrustes alignment. We evaluate our model on the SemEval-2020 Task 1, specifically on the English subtask 2, which concerns ranking a set of 37 target words according to their semantic change between two time periods. Our results are assessed using Spearman's rank-order correlation coefficient, and we note the caveat that our model is fine-tuned on Urban Dictionary text, while the older of the two English datasets consists of text from 1810. This work contributes to the ongoing efforts to develop effective methods for lexical semantic change detection, a field that has seen considerable developments in recent years.
gold_abstract:
Recent NLP architectures have illustrated in various ways how semantic change
can be captured across time and domains. However, in terms of evaluation there
is a lack of benchmarks to compare the performance of these systems against
each other. We present the results of the first shared task on unsupervised
lexical semantic change detection (LSCD) in German based on the evaluation
framework proposed by Schlechtweg et al. (2019).

title: Sparsity-Accelerated Training for Large Language Models
generated_abstract: Sparsity-Accelerated Training for Large Language Models Recent years have seen the rise of transformer-based language models, achieving state-of-the-art results across various NLP tasks, such as machine translation and question answering. However, their extensive training requirements and computational costs pose significant challenges for deployment in resource-constrained environments. To address these limitations, we propose Sparsity-Accelerated Training (SAT), a novel approach to reduce the computational cost of training transformer language models. By iteratively pruning and fine-tuning the model, SAT effectively accelerates the training process while maintaining model performance, as demonstrated by its application to the transformer language model. In comparison to other compression methods, SAT offers a more efficient solution for large-scale language models.
gold_abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated proficiency across various natural language processing (NLP) tasks but often require additional training, such as continual pre-training and supervised fine-tuning. However, the costs associated with this, primarily due to their large parameter count, remain high. This paper proposes leveraging sparsity in pre-trained LLMs to expedite this training process. By observing sparsity in activated neurons during forward iterations, we identify the potential for computational speed-ups by excluding inactive neurons. We address associated challenges by extending existing neuron importance evaluation metrics and introducing a ladder omission rate scheduler. Our experiments on Llama-2 demonstrate that Sparsity-Accelerated Training (SAT) achieves comparable or superior performance to standard training while significantly accelerating the process. Specifically, SAT achieves a [MATH] throughput improvement in continual pre-training and saves [MATH] training time in supervised fine-tuning in practice. It offers a simple, hardware-agnostic, and easily deployable framework for additional LLM training. Our code is available at {https://github.com/OpenDFM/SAT}.

title: Checks and Strategies for Enabling Code-Switched Machine Translation
generated_abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of code-switched machine translation, a pressing task in NLP due to the growing prevalence of code-switching among multilingual speakers. The task of translating into code-switched language is challenging, as evidenced by the lack of standard datasets for this task, and has garnered attention in recent research, including the creation of the CSTOP dataset containing 5800 Spanglish utterances (Baheti et al., 2021). To address this challenge, we propose checks and strategies for enabling code-switched machine translation, including data augmentation methods, concatenation, and adding noise to improve robustness. Our approach draws from existing work on code-switched data, such as the use of discriminative training for language models (Baheti et al., 2021) and the creation of comprehensive datasets for machine translation (Rajaraman et al., 2020). By leveraging these approaches, we aim to improve the performance of code-switched machine translation models.
gold_abstract:
Code-switching is a common phenomenon among multilingual speakers, where
alternation between two or more languages occurs within the context of a single
conversation. While multilingual humans can seamlessly switch back and forth
between languages, multilingual neural machine translation (NMT) models are not
robust to such sudden changes in input. This work explores multilingual NMT
models' ability to handle code-switched text. First, we propose checks to
measure switching capability. Second, we investigate simple and effective data
augmentation methods that can enhance an NMT model's ability to support
code-switching. Finally, by using a glass-box analysis of attention modules, we
demonstrate the effectiveness of these methods in improving robustness.

title: Incorporating Task-specific Concept Knowledge into Script Learning
generated_abstract: Incorporating Task-specific Concept Knowledge into Script Learning addresses the challenge of generating high-quality scripts from narratives by leveraging pre-trained language models. Our work aligns with the goal-oriented script paradigm, which outlines the steps needed to achieve a specific goal, as seen in prior research on script classification. To investigate how well pre-trained language models have captured script knowledge, we design three probing tasks, including inclusive sub-event selection, starting sub-event selection, and sub-event temporal ordering. In contrast to general knowledge stored in pre-trained language models, our method incorporates task-specific concept knowledge to improve script generation, inspired by cognitive theories of learning and knowledge-augmented language models. This approach has the potential to improve script generation performance, especially for domain-specific tasks, where contextualizing texts with domain knowledge is more effective. By leveraging task-specific concept knowledge, our method aims to improve the quality of generated scripts, similar to the incorporation of shallow syntactic information in downstream tasks.
gold_abstract:
In this paper, we present Tetris, a new task of Goal-Oriented Script
Completion. Unlike previous work, it considers a more realistic and more
general setting, where the input includes not only the goal but also additional
user context, including preferences and history. To address the problem using a
knowledge-based approach, we introduce Task Concept Graph, an automatically
constructed knowledge base from instructional websites. Different from
Commonsense Knowledge Base like ConceptNet, the task concept graph schema
introduces various types of noun phrases-based nodes specifically for
accomplishing a task. To integrate such graphs into script learning, we devise
two methods that acquire concepts from the knowledge base as prompts to
downstream script completion. On our WikiHow-based dataset, we find that
incorporating concepts from the Task Concept Graph consistently improves
performance, demonstrating the benefit of Task Concept Graph for this task.
Furthermore, the model with gold-standard concepts as prompt outperforms the
baseline significantly, further confirming the need for task-specific knowledge
in goal-oriented script completion. The dataset, repository, models, and demo
will be publicly available to facilitate further research on this new task.

title:
Pragmatic constraints and pronoun reference disambiguation: the possible
  and the impossible
generated_abstract: This paper explores the complex relationships between semantics, context, and pronoun disambiguation, highlighting the importance of understanding pragmatic constraints in natural language processing. Drawing on evidence from the evaluation of pronoun translation in machine translation (MT), we examine the nuances of pronoun reference, including antecedents that are entirely missing and demonstrative pronouns referring to a category. We also consider the challenges of remote pronoun antecedents in literature and the importance of excluding forms that do not conform to standard pronoun usage. Our analysis is grounded in empirical studies of coreference resolution and the use of contextualized representations to encode information about pronouns' antecedents. Ultimately, we argue that a more inclusive approach to pronoun handling is necessary to accommodate the evolving nature of language and provide genuinely inclusive systems.
gold_abstract:
Pronoun disambiguation in understanding text and discourse often requires the
application of both general pragmatic knowledge and context-specific
information. In AI and linguistics research, this has mostly been studied in
cases where the referent is explicitly stated in the preceding text nearby.
However, pronouns in natural text often refer to entities, collections, or
events that are only implicitly mentioned previously; in those cases the need
to use pragmatic knowledge to disambiguate becomes much more acute and the
characterization of the knowledge becomes much more difficult. Extended
literary texts at times employ both extremely complex patterns of reference and
extremely rich and subtle forms of knowledge. Indeed, it is occasionally
possible to have a pronoun that is far separated from its referent in a text.
In the opposite direction, pronoun use is affected by considerations of focus
of attention and by formal constraints such as a preference for parallel
syntactic structures; these can be so strong that no pragmatic knowledge
suffices to overrule them.
